analysis_summary: 51 confirmed finding(s) generated by Scheme B chain-context analysis.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper reset handler initializes acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), which sets all access control permission bits to fully permissive on reset, rather than a secure default of all-zeros.

evidence.description: Lines 81-85: On reset (when ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) is true), acct_mem[j] is set to 32'hffffffff for all j. Since acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem (see SNIP-0007 line 49), this means all access control permission bits default to 1 (fully permitted) after reset rather than a secure locked-down state of 0.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes permission memory to all-ones, granting all access by default.

evidence.description: Line 49: acc_ctrl_o is assigned directly from acct_mem entries. A reset value of 0xFFFFFFFF in acct_mem propagates to acc_ctrl_o, making all access control outputs fully permissive immediately after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control output signal derives from the all-ones reset state of acct_mem.

evidence.description: Line 222: acc_ctrl_c is computed from acc_ctrl (which receives acc_ctrl_o from acct_wrapper). With acc_ctrl_o all-ones after reset, acc_ctrl_c will also be all-ones, meaning all peripherals are fully accessible at all privilege levels.
evidence.supports_claim: All-ones acc_ctrl_o propagates through acc_ctrl_c to affect all peripheral access control decisions.

evidence.description: Line 517: rom_req is gated by acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]. If acc_ctrl_c is all-ones after reset, the bootrom is accessible regardless of privilege level, and the commented-out line 516 shows this guard was previously disabled entirely, indicating awareness of the bypass risk.
evidence.supports_claim: All-permissive reset state of acc_ctrl_c allows bootrom access without privilege enforcement during and shortly after reset.

evidence.description: Line 926: rst_wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12]. With all-ones acc_ctrl_c after acct_wrapper reset, the rst_wrapper access control input is also 1 (permitted), meaning the reset controller is accessible immediately post-reset.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset controller peripheral is unprotected immediately after reset due to the all-ones default of acct_mem.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module stores access control permissions in acct_mem. On reset, this memory is initialized to 32'hffffffff (all bits set), meaning every access control bit defaults to 'permit'. The output acc_ctrl_o is derived from acct_mem, and acc_ctrl_c is computed from acc_ctrl_o in riscv_peripherals. Since acc_ctrl_c gates access to all peripherals including the bootrom and reset controller, any window of time where software can interact with the system before the ACCT registers are explicitly re-programmed will see fully permissive access control. A secure-by-default design should initialize permission memory to all-zeros (deny all), requiring explicit grants rather than relying on post-reset re-programming to restrict access.
findings.security_impact: After any reset, all peripheral access controls default to fully permitted. An attacker or unprivileged software that executes before the access control table is re-programmed (e.g., due to a partial reset, a race condition during boot, or a deliberate rst_6 assertion) gains unrestricted access to all protected peripherals, including the reset controller (rst_wrapper), bootrom, and all other resources managed by acct_wrapper. This can lead to privilege escalation, unauthorized code execution, and circumvention of the entire hardware access control policy.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact timing of when software re-programs acct_mem after reset is not visible in RTL alone; however, the hardware-level default is clearly insecure regardless of software behavior. The we_flag signal (line 49, SNIP-0007) adds an OR with {8{we_flag}} to acct_mem[0] bits, which could further override permissions, but its driver is not fully analyzed here.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper module resets acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-access-permitted) on reset, and the acc_ctrl_o output unconditionally ORs a we_flag into the lowest permission bits, potentially bypassing access control restrictions.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni or rst_6 asserted), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. This means every peripheral is granted full access permissions at reset, rather than defaulting to a locked/deny-all state.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes access control memory to all-ones (fully permissive), creating a window where all access is permitted until software explicitly restricts it.

evidence.description: Line 49: 'assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};' unconditionally ORs we_flag into the lowest 8 bits of acc_ctrl_o, regardless of any lock (reglk_ctrl) or privilege check. This allows the we_flag input to forcibly set permission bits in acc_ctrl_o even when reglk_ctrl would otherwise prevent modification of acct_mem.
evidence.supports_claim: The we_flag signal can bypass the register-lock protection for read access-control output, granting broader permissions than stored in acct_mem.

evidence.description: Write access to acct_mem is gated by 'en && we' and reglk_ctrl bits. Read access is gated by 'en' and reglk_ctrl bits. However, the acc_ctrl_o assignment on line 49 does not respect reglk_ctrl for the we_flag OR, meaning the output permission signal can be elevated beyond what is locked in memory.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_ctrl guards only direct read/write of acct_mem registers, but does not protect the acc_ctrl_o output from being inflated by we_flag.

evidence.description: acct_mem stores per-peripheral access control values. acc_ctrl_o is derived from acct_mem but with we_flag bits unconditionally OR'd in. The reglk_ctrl lock bits are checked only during reads/writes of acct_mem, not when forming acc_ctrl_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The permission state exported to the system (acc_ctrl_o) can differ from the locked-down state in acct_mem due to we_flag injection.

evidence.description: Line 517: 'assign rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0];' uses acc_ctrl_c derived from acc_ctrl_o to gate bootrom access. If acc_ctrl_o has permission bits artificially set by we_flag, lower privilege levels may gain bootrom access they should not have.
evidence.supports_claim: Inflated acc_ctrl_o values propagate to peripheral access decisions including bootrom gating, potentially allowing unauthorized access.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module stores access control values in acct_mem, which is properly guarded by register-lock bits (reglk_ctrl) for read and write operations. However, the output acc_ctrl_o is formed by unconditionally OR-ing we_flag into the lowest permission word (line 49), bypassing the reglk_ctrl protection entirely for the exported permission signal. Additionally, on reset, acct_mem is initialized to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-permissive), which is an unsafe default. These two issues together mean: (1) the system is fully open at reset until software closes it, and (2) the we_flag input can permanently elevate exported permissions regardless of lock state.
findings.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software that can control we_flag can cause acc_ctrl_o to grant access permissions to peripherals regardless of the configured lock state. The unsafe reset default also creates a window after every reset where all peripherals are accessible to all privilege levels, which could be exploited during boot sequences.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact source and privilege requirements of we_flag are not shown in the provided snippets; its trust level is unclear. It is also unclear whether we_flag is always tied to a fixed value in production use or can be dynamically controlled. The full instantiation context of acct_wrapper showing the we_flag connection is not included in the snippets.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper module resets acct_mem to all-ones (0xffffffff) on reset, which initializes access control entries to a fully permissive state. Additionally, the reset condition uses a compound expression (~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)) that could allow rst_6 to trigger a reset and restore the permissive default state, potentially bypassing locked access control entries.

evidence.description: On reset (~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)), all acct_mem entries are initialized to 32'hffffffff (all ones). This means the access control memory defaults to a fully permissive state after any reset event, including when rst_6 is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes the access control table to all-ones (fully permissive), meaning any reset event restores maximum access permissions regardless of previously locked values.

evidence.description: The reset condition is `if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6))`, meaning that asserting rst_6 alone (even without deasserting rst_ni) triggers a reset of acct_mem to 0xffffffff. rst_6 is an external input signal, and any entity that can assert rst_6 can reset the access control table to fully permissive values, bypassing any register locks (reglk_ctrl) that were in place.
evidence.supports_claim: An attacker or software with control over rst_6 can reset acct_mem to all-ones, effectively granting full access permissions to all peripherals and bypassing the register lock protection.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i (register lock values) and acct_ctrl_i to guard access. The acc_ctrl_o output is derived from acct_mem and directly controls peripheral access permissions.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem is the protected resource holding access control state; resetting it to 0xffffffff undermines the security policy enforced by reglk_ctrl.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper produces rst_6 as one of its outputs. The rst_wrapper itself receives debug_mode_i and has write access via AXI. This creates a chain where debug or privileged AXI writes to rst_wrapper can produce rst_6, which then resets acct_mem to all-ones in acct_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 originates from rst_wrapper which is influenced by debug_mode_i, establishing a debug/reset path that can force acct_mem back to a fully permissive state.

evidence.description: acct_mem entries control access permissions for peripherals (NB_SLAVE interfaces). The write side uses reglk_ctrl bits to lock individual entries, but the reset path unconditionally overwrites all entries with 0xffffffff regardless of lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock mechanism is bypassed by the reset path, which does not respect reglk_ctrl when resetting acct_mem to all-ones.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper stores access control entries in acct_mem, which is protected by reglk_ctrl bits during normal writes. However, the reset logic uses the condition `~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)`, which triggers whenever rst_6 is asserted (independently of rst_ni). When triggered, all acct_mem entries are set to 32'hffffffff (all permissions granted). Since rst_6 is an output of rst_wrapper, which is AXI-accessible and influenced by debug_mode_i, any entity with write access to rst_wrapper or debug mode control can indirectly trigger the reset of acct_mem, restoring all-permissions state and bypassing any register locks that were set. This is a concrete security vulnerability: the register lock protection (reglk_ctrl) on acct_mem writes is circumvented by the reset path.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with access to the rst_wrapper AXI interface or debug mode can assert rst_6, causing acct_wrapper to reset all access control entries to 0xffffffff. This grants all peripherals full access permissions, effectively nullifying the entire access control policy. All previously locked access control registers become unlocked and set to maximum permissions, allowing unauthorized access to all protected peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact mechanism by which rst_6 is asserted (i.e., the full rst_wrapper write path) is not fully shown in the snippets, and the precise privilege level required to write to rst_wrapper via AXI is not visible in the provided code. However, the reset-to-all-ones behavior and the rst_6 dependency are clearly visible in SNIP-0005.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper read-side access control uses only reglk_ctrl[0] for all six register slots (indices 0-5), meaning a single lock bit gates all register lock memory reads. More critically, the acct_wrapper uses different reglk_ctrl bits for different address ranges but those bits are drawn from the same reglk_ctrl_i input that is also controlled by the reglk_wrapper itself, and the rst_wrapper read path only checks reglk_ctrl_i[0] for the single readable register. The core vulnerability is in reglk_wrapper: all reglk_mem[0..5] reads are protected solely by reglk_ctrl[0] (bit 0 of reglk_ctrl_i), so if bit 0 is not set, all six lock-control memory slots are readable regardless of any per-register intent. There is no privilege check, no acct_ctrl check, and no debug_mode check on the reglk read path, unlike rst_wrapper which at least accepts those inputs.

evidence.description: All six reglk_mem slots (indices 0-5) are gated by the same single bit reglk_ctrl[0]. If bit 0 of reglk_ctrl_i is 0, all register-lock configuration values are readable via AXI. There is no per-register lock bit, no privilege/acct check, and no debug_mode gate on the read path.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing per-register access guard on reglk_wrapper read path; single bit controls readback of all lock configuration registers.

evidence.description: reglk_mem holds register lock values output as reglk_ctrl_o, which controls access to all peripherals. Reading these values leaks the system's lock configuration.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem contains security-critical lock state; its readback must be properly guarded.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper read path uses different reglk_ctrl bits (bits 4, 2, 0, 6) for different address ranges but the guard depends entirely on bits from reglk_ctrl_i, which is sourced from reglk_wrapper output. If reglk_wrapper itself is readable (due to missing guard), an attacker could learn the lock state before it is enforced.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_wrapper guard logic depends on reglk_ctrl_i, which itself is insufficiently protected in reglk_wrapper.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper read path checks reglk_ctrl_i[0] for its single readable register, consistent with other wrappers, but the debug_mode_i input accepted by rst_wrapper (SNIP-0011) is not used in the read guard, leaving a potential debug-mode bypass if the logic is incomplete.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_wrapper accepts debug_mode_i but does not apply it on the read path, potentially allowing debug-mode register readback without additional privilege check.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper accepts debug_mode_i as an input port, but the read-side logic in SNIP-0013 only checks reglk_ctrl_i[0] without considering debug_mode_i, so debug mode does not grant nor restrict additional read access through an explicit guard.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i input is accepted but unused in read access control, creating an inconsistency in the security model.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals and its AXI response is wired out, making the register lock memory accessible via the AXI bus to any bus master that can address the reglk peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI-accessible entry point for reglk_wrapper with insufficient read guards exposes lock configuration to bus masters.

findings.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module stores and outputs register lock control values (reglk_ctrl_o) that govern access to all other peripherals. Its read-side logic (SNIP-0010) uses a single lock bit (reglk_ctrl[0]) to guard all six reglk_mem slots. If this bit is zero (unlocked), any AXI master can read all lock configuration values, leaking the full security policy. Additionally, rst_wrapper accepts debug_mode_i but does not factor it into the read guard, creating an inconsistency. The acct_wrapper and rst_wrapper read guards depend on reglk_ctrl_i sourced from reglk_wrapper, so the weakness in reglk_wrapper's self-protection propagates to the trust assumptions of all other wrappers.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI bus master can read all register lock configuration values from reglk_wrapper when reglk_ctrl_i[0] is 0 (default/unlocked state), revealing the full peripheral access control policy. This enables an attacker to enumerate which registers are locked and which are not, facilitating targeted attacks on unlocked peripherals. Additionally, the rst_wrapper's debug_mode_i is accepted but not enforced on reads, potentially allowing unintended register readback in debug mode.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The initial value of reglk_ctrl_i[0] at boot is not shown in the provided snippets; if it defaults to 1 (locked), the read-path vulnerability would only be exploitable after an attacker unlocks it. However, the structural absence of per-register or per-privilege guards is a concrete design deficiency regardless of reset state. The debug_mode_i unused-in-read-guard finding has medium confidence as the full rst_wrapper write-side logic is not shown.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper reset handler initializes acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF) on reset, which sets all access control bits to fully permissive. A software-triggered reset via rst_6 (from rst_wrapper) can restore this open-access state, effectively bypassing any previously locked access control configuration.

evidence.description: On reset (when ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. This means every access control bit is set to 1, granting maximum permissions to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes the access control memory to fully permissive state, so any reset event restores open access.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper outputs rst_6 which is connected to the acct_wrapper's rst_6 input. The rst_wrapper is a software-accessible module (it has an AXI interface), allowing software to potentially trigger rst_6.
evidence.supports_claim: A software-controllable reset signal (rst_6) can trigger the acct_wrapper reset path that sets acct_mem to all-ones.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals and receives acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12], meaning access to the reset controller is gated by privilege level and access control. However, if access control itself is reset to all-permissive, this protection can be circumvented.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset controller that can trigger acct_wrapper reset is itself protected by the same access control system being reset.

evidence.description: acct_mem stores access control values that gate access to all peripherals. On reset it is set to 0xFFFFFFFF (all permissions granted). The acc_ctrl_o output derived from acct_mem is used to gate peripheral access system-wide.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control memory is the protected resource that enforces security policy; resetting it to all-ones removes all access restrictions.

evidence.description: Line 84: acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff in the reset branch. The use of 32'hffffffff as the reset value for access control registers is a security violation—after any rst_6 assertion, all access control restrictions are cleared.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset-to-permissive behavior means that any entity capable of asserting rst_6 (including potentially unprivileged software that gains access to the rst_wrapper AXI interface) can restore full peripheral access, defeating the purpose of the access control table.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper instantiation in riscv_peripherals shows it controls acc_ctrl_o used for acc_ctrl and acc_ctrl_c signals, which gate access to all 14 NB_PERIPHERALS. A reset to all-ones grants full access to every peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: Resetting acct_mem to all-ones compromises access control for all peripherals in the system.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module maintains an access control table (acct_mem) that enforces per-peripheral access policies. On reset—including a software-triggerable rst_6 from the rst_wrapper module—acct_mem is initialized to 0xFFFFFFFF, setting all access control bits to 1 (fully permissive). This means any reset event reverts the system to an open-access state, bypassing previously programmed restrictions. The rst_wrapper is itself AXI-accessible, and while it is gated by acct_ctrl_i, the circular dependency means a successful reset of acct_mem would allow re-access to the rst_wrapper to trigger further resets. The initialization value should be 0 (deny-all) or a secure default, not all-ones.
findings.security_impact: An attacker who can trigger rst_6 (via the AXI-accessible rst_wrapper) can reset the entire access control table to all-permissive, granting themselves and other software unrestricted access to all 14 peripherals including security-critical ones such as AES, SHA256, HMAC, and PKT modules.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege requirements to access rst_wrapper at runtime are not fully visible from the provided snippets. It is possible that rst_6 can only be asserted by sufficiently privileged software, which would reduce exploitability. However, the unsafe reset-to-permissive behavior is clearly present in the source code regardless of the trigger path.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper resets all acct_mem entries to 0xFFFFFFFF (fully permissive) on both rst_ni deassertion and rst_6 assertion, and the read-lock bits in reglk_ctrl used to gate acct_mem readback are misaligned with the write-lock bits, creating a window where access-control state is fully open after any reset and where the lock-bit indices used for reads differ from those used for writes, potentially allowing unauthorized reads or writes of access-control table entries.

evidence.description: Lines 81-85: on reset (either ~rst_ni or rst_6 asserted), all acct_mem entries are set to 32'hffffffff. This means every access-control bit is set to '1' (fully permissive or fully set depending on polarity), so a reset event leaves the access-control table in a maximally open state rather than a locked/safe default.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset drives acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), which is an unsafe reset value for a security-critical access-control memory.

evidence.description: Write path (lines 88-111) uses reglk_ctrl[5] to lock acct_mem[00..02], reglk_ctrl[13] for acct_mem[03..05], reglk_ctrl[1] for acct_mem[06..08], reglk_ctrl[7] for acct_mem[09]. Read path (lines 118-143) uses reglk_ctrl[4] for acct_mem[0..2], reglk_ctrl[2] for acct_mem[3..5], reglk_ctrl[0] for acct_mem[6..8], reglk_ctrl[6] for acct_mem[9]. The read-lock bit indices do not match the write-lock bit indices for the same memory regions.
evidence.supports_claim: Mismatched lock-bit indices between read and write paths mean a lock that blocks writes does not necessarily block reads, and vice versa, violating consistent protection of the same resource.

evidence.description: Line 51: reglk_ctrl is simply assigned directly from reglk_ctrl_i with no additional qualification or masking. This means the full external input directly controls all lock bits with no internal hardening.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock register is a transparent passthrough from the external input, so any corruption or manipulation of reglk_ctrl_i immediately affects all lock decisions.

evidence.description: The write guard at line 90 uses reglk_ctrl[5] while the read guard at line 121 uses reglk_ctrl[4] for the same acct_mem[0..2] region. This asymmetry means setting bit 5 (write-lock) does not prevent readback, and setting bit 4 (read-lock) does not prevent writes. An attacker who can influence only one lock bit can therefore either read locked data or write to read-protected registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent read/write lock bit indices for the same acct_mem regions constitute a concrete missing-guard vulnerability on either the read or write path.

evidence.description: Lines 49: acc_ctrl_o is directly derived from acct_mem values ORed with we_flag, and is distributed to all peripherals as their access-control values. Corruption of acct_mem (via incorrect locking) directly affects system-wide access control.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem is the root of the access-control policy for NB_PERIPHERALS peripherals; incorrect protection allows privilege escalation across the entire peripheral fabric.

findings.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present in acct_wrapper. First, the reset behavior sets acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-ones), which is an unsafe default for a security-critical access-control table—if bits represent 'allow', all accesses are permitted after reset. Second, and more critically, the read-lock bit indices (reglk_ctrl[4], [2], [0], [6]) differ from the write-lock bit indices (reglk_ctrl[5], [13], [1], [7]) for the same memory regions. This means a caller can write to a 'write-locked' region by exploiting only the read-lock bits being set (or vice versa). These are directly evidenced in the source snippets showing the case statements for reads and writes.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access can read back access-control table entries that should be locked (if write-lock bit is set but read-lock bit is not), or write to regions that should be protected (if read-lock bit is set but write-lock bit is not). Since acct_mem drives acc_ctrl_o which controls peripheral access for the entire RISC-V SoC, this could enable privilege escalation or bypass of peripheral access controls system-wide. The unsafe reset value additionally means any reset event temporarily grants maximum access to all peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of each acct_mem bit (whether '1' means allow or deny) are not fully documented in the provided snippets, which affects the precise characterization of the reset-value risk. However, the mismatched read/write lock-bit indices are unambiguously present in the source code and constitute a definitive finding regardless of bit polarity.

findings.summary: In reglk_wrapper, writing to address index 2 (case 2) incorrectly assigns reglk_mem[3] (instead of reglk_mem[2]) as the retained value when the lock bit is set, causing reglk_mem[2] to be written with wdata even when locked, while also corrupting the held-value logic.

evidence.description: reglk_mem is the register-lock memory array whose contents are broadcast as reglk_ctrl_o to all peripherals. Correct protection of its entries is security-critical.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem stores per-peripheral lock bits; unauthorized writes here affect all downstream permission checks.

evidence.description: Lines 88-99: write-side case statement uses reglk_ctrl[3] for index 0, and reglk_ctrl[1] for indices 1-5. Case 2 at line 93 reads: `reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;` — when the lock bit is SET it substitutes reglk_mem[3] (a different register) instead of reglk_mem[2], meaning the guarded value is wrong. Moreover when the lock bit is CLEAR it still writes wdata into reglk_mem[2] normally, but the 'locked' path uses the wrong source register.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard for reglk_mem[2] is broken: it does not preserve reglk_mem[2] when locked; instead it copies reglk_mem[3] into reglk_mem[2], corrupting the lock state.

evidence.description: Line 93: `reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;` — the correct pattern used by all other cases is `reglk_mem[N] <= reglk_ctrl[x] ? reglk_mem[N] : wdata` (self-retention). Here reglk_mem[3] is used instead of reglk_mem[2] in the locked branch, so: (a) reglk_mem[2] is always overwritten (either by reglk_mem[3] when locked or by wdata when unlocked), and (b) the lock is effectively bypassed for the true value of reglk_mem[2] because writing with lock set silently replaces it with reglk_mem[3].
evidence.supports_claim: This is a concrete incorrect protection state update: the lock bit for reglk_mem[2] does not prevent modification of that register; it only changes which source corrupts it.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper.reglk_ctrl_o feeds the system-wide reglk_ctrl bus (line 1817), which is distributed to all peripheral wrappers including acct_wrapper and rst_wrapper. Corruption of reglk_mem[2] can alter the lock bits seen by multiple peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: A corrupted reglk_mem[2] propagates incorrect lock values to all peripherals via reglk_ctrl_o, potentially enabling reads or writes to otherwise locked peripheral registers.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl bits 0,2,4,5,6,13 to gate reads and writes to acct_mem. If the wrong lock value is delivered (because reglk_mem[2] is corrupted), access-control entries may become readable or writable by unprivileged software.
evidence.supports_claim: Downstream peripherals relying on the lock values derived from reglk_mem[2] will enforce incorrect permissions.

findings.reasoning_summary: In reglk_wrapper's write-side always block, case index 2 contains a copy-paste error: the locked-branch retention value is reglk_mem[3] instead of reglk_mem[2]. All other cases correctly self-retain (e.g., `reglk_mem[0] <= reglk_ctrl[3] ? reglk_mem[0] : wdata`). Because of this mistake, whenever the lock bit is asserted for index 2, the register is still modified (overwritten with reglk_mem[3]), bypassing the intended immutability guarantee. The reglk_mem array is the root source of all peripheral lock signals distributed system-wide via reglk_ctrl_o, making this a security-relevant incorrect protection state update.
findings.security_impact: An attacker who can write to the reglk peripheral register at address index 2 while the lock bit is asserted can still corrupt reglk_mem[2] (it receives reglk_mem[3] instead of being preserved). Additionally, even normal locked operation silently overwrites reglk_mem[2] with reglk_mem[3], which may unlock or alter lock bits controlling other peripherals. This could allow unauthorized read or write access to otherwise locked security-sensitive registers in acct_wrapper, rst_wrapper, and other peripherals controlled by the affected lock bits.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact bit-field mapping of reglk_mem[2] to specific peripheral lock bits is not fully detailed in the snippets, so the precise downstream peripheral affected is inferred from the reglk_ctrl_o packing formula. No additional ambiguity about the source-code defect itself.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper module resets acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF) when rst_6 is asserted, which is a software-controllable reset signal generated by rst_wrapper from a memory-mapped register. This allows any privileged software that can write to the rst_ctrl peripheral to force the access-control table (acct_mem) into a maximally permissive state (all bits set), bypassing the normal register-lock protections.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper drives rst_6 directly from rst_mem[6], which is loaded from a memory-mapped AXI-accessible register. rst_6 is described as the reset signal for the ACCT peripheral, meaning software can assert it by writing to the rst_ctrl peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is a software-writable signal that can be asserted at will via the rst_ctrl AXI interface.

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, the reset condition `if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6))` causes acct_mem[j] to be set to 32'hffffffff for all entries. All-ones in acct_mem means all access-control permissions are granted. Because rst_6 is software-controllable, any software that can write to rst_ctrl can force acct_mem to this maximally permissive state, overriding the register-lock guards.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting software-controlled rst_6 clears acct_mem to all-ones, granting maximum access permissions and bypassing reglk_ctrl protection.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem entries. When acct_mem is all-ones due to rst_6 assertion, acc_ctrl_o broadcasts full permissions to all 14 peripherals in riscv_peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem is the protected resource controlling per-peripheral access; its reset value of all-ones makes all peripherals accessible.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with reglk and acct_ctrl guards, but rst_6 output is connected directly to acct_wrapper without any additional filtering, meaning software writing to rst_ctrl can assert rst_6 targeting the ACCT module.
evidence.supports_claim: The rst_6 signal flows from a software-writable register in rst_wrapper directly into acct_wrapper's reset path.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with rst_6 connected (edge:connects:28). The acct_wrapper uses rst_6 as part of its reset condition, confirming the software-to-reset-to-acct_mem path.
evidence.supports_claim: The connection of rst_6 from riscv_peripherals to acct_wrapper confirms the software-controlled reset path into the access control table.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper's AXI interface allows software to write rst_mem entries (including rst_mem[6] for ACCT) via normal AXI write transactions. No snippet shows that writing to rst_mem[6] requires a privilege level beyond what the reglk_ctrl_i for slot 12 provides, and the reset of acct_mem itself is not protected by reglk_ctrl.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: the reset path that sets acct_mem to all-ones is not protected by reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl checks.

findings.reasoning_summary: The rst_6 signal in acct_wrapper is driven by rst_wrapper's rst_mem[6], which is loaded from a software-writable AXI register in the rst_ctrl peripheral. When rst_6 is asserted, acct_wrapper resets its entire acct_mem array to 32'hffffffff (all-ones), which represents maximum permissions for all access-control entries. The normal write-protection mechanism (reglk_ctrl) only guards individual acct_mem entries during normal operation, but does not protect against the reset path. An attacker with sufficient privilege to write to the rst_ctrl peripheral can assert rst_6, forcing acct_mem to all-ones and thereby granting all peripherals maximum access permissions, regardless of the intended lock state.
findings.security_impact: An attacker who can write to the rst_ctrl peripheral can assert rst_6 to reset the ACCT access-control table to all-ones, granting unrestricted access to all 14 peripherals in the system. This completely undermines the access-control enforcement that acct_wrapper is designed to provide, potentially allowing unauthorized access to security-sensitive peripherals such as AES, SHA, HMAC, and others.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to write to the rst_ctrl peripheral is not shown in the snippets. If rst_ctrl writes require machine-mode or a high privilege level that already implies full access, the practical exploitability may be reduced. However, the structural vulnerability (software reset causing all-ones in the access-control table) is clearly evidenced in the source.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper reset condition sets all access-control memory entries to 0xFFFFFFFF (fully permissive), and the reset can be triggered by the rst_6 signal driven by rst_wrapper, which is software-controllable. This allows a privileged (or unauthorized) software agent to reset the ACCT peripheral and restore all access-control registers to a maximally permissive state, bypassing any previously configured access restrictions.

evidence.description: On reset (when rst_ni is deasserted OR rst_6 is asserted), all acct_mem entries are set to 32'hffffffff, meaning all access-control bits are set to '1' (fully permissive). The condition `~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)` means that asserting rst_6 alone is sufficient to reset the entire access-control memory to all-ones.
evidence.supports_claim: A software-triggered rst_6 pulse resets all access-control registers to 0xFFFFFFFF, erasing any prior access restrictions and granting maximum permissions to all peripherals.

evidence.description: rst_6 is assigned from rst_mem[6] in rst_wrapper, which is the ACCT reset line. The rst_wrapper exposes rst_6 as a direct output derived from a software-writable register (rst_mem), providing a mechanism to assert rst_6 independently of the global rst_ni.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is software-controllable via the rst_wrapper AXI-accessible register interface, enabling an attacker with write access to rst_wrapper to trigger an ACCT reset at will.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i protection inputs, but the rst_6 output feeds directly into acct_wrapper's rst_6 input, creating a path from a software-writable register to the ACCT reset.
evidence.supports_claim: The rst_6 signal flows from rst_wrapper (AXI-accessible) into acct_wrapper's reset logic, confirming the software-reachable reset path to ACCT memory.

evidence.description: acct_mem holds per-peripheral access-control values. The acc_ctrl_o output is derived directly from acct_mem entries and governs what accesses are permitted to each peripheral. Resetting acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF makes acc_ctrl_o maximally permissive.
evidence.supports_claim: The access-control memory (acct_mem) is the security-critical resource; its reset value of all-ones bypasses all access restrictions, constituting a security-relevant unsafe reset behavior.

evidence.description: The rst_wrapper write logic at lines 107-109 shows that if the start condition is not met, rst_mem is set to 0 (no resets active). However, when a write to the appropriate address sets rst_mem[6], rst_6 is asserted, and there is no evidence of privilege-level gating or lock enforcement on the rst_wrapper write path itself within this snippet.
evidence.supports_claim: The rst_wrapper does not visibly enforce privilege-level or lock-bit checks before allowing software to assert rst_6, leaving the reset path inadequately guarded.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper initializes all access-control memory (acct_mem) to 0xFFFFFFFF on any reset event, including when rst_6 is asserted. The rst_6 signal is generated by rst_wrapper from a software-writable register (rst_mem[6]) accessible via AXI. The instantiation in riscv_peripherals confirms this path. Therefore, software that can write to rst_wrapper's register space can trigger a targeted reset of ACCT, setting all access-control registers to the maximally permissive value and bypassing any security policy previously programmed into acct_mem. The reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl_i inputs protect writes and reads to acct_mem in normal operation, but they do not prevent the reset from overwriting acct_mem with all-ones.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with AXI write access to the rst_wrapper peripheral can assert rst_6, which causes acct_wrapper to reset all access-control registers to 0xFFFFFFFF. This grants maximum access permissions for all peripherals governed by acc_ctrl_o, completely bypassing the intended access-control policy and potentially enabling unauthorized access to security-sensitive peripherals (AES, SHA, HMAC, etc.).
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege-level enforcement on writes to rst_wrapper is not fully visible in the provided snippets (acct_ctrl_i gating logic for rst_wrapper writes is not shown in SNIP-0013). However, the unsafe reset value of 0xFFFFFFFF in acct_mem and the software-controllable rst_6 path are clearly evidenced.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper reset path initializes acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF per entry), which grants maximum access control permissions on reset, rather than a safe default of all-zeros. This means that after any reset event (including soft resets triggered via rst_wrapper), all access control entries are maximally permissive until explicitly programmed.

evidence.description: On reset (when rst_ni is deasserted or rst_6 is asserted), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. Since acct_mem holds access control values, all-ones is a maximally permissive state, not a safe locked-down default.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset sets acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), granting all access permissions rather than denying them by default.

evidence.description: acct_mem is the access control memory whose contents are exported as acc_ctrl_o and used to gate access to peripherals. The reset value directly determines what is permitted immediately after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem controls access permissions for peripherals; its reset value determines the default security posture.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper can assert rst_6 (the ACCT module reset line) based on AXI writes, meaning an attacker who can write to the rst_wrapper registers could trigger a reset of acct_mem back to the all-ones (fully permissive) state.
evidence.supports_claim: A soft reset triggered via rst_wrapper causes acct_mem to revert to fully permissive state, bypassing any previously programmed access control restrictions.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper exposes AXI-lite write access to control reset lines including rst_6, which resets the acct_mem. The guard logic in rst_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i but the reset path itself reinitializes acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI writes to rst_wrapper can trigger acct_mem reset, restoring the maximally permissive state.

evidence.description: By contrast, reglk_wrapper resets reglk_mem to 0 on reset, which is a safe deny-all default. The asymmetry confirms acct_wrapper's all-ones reset is an intentional but insecure design choice.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem resets to 0 (safe default) while acct_mem resets to 0xFFFFFFFF (unsafe default), demonstrating inconsistent security posture.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module initializes its access control memory (acct_mem) to 32'hffffffff on reset. Since acct_mem gates peripheral access via acc_ctrl_o, this reset value represents a fully permissive state. An adversary who can trigger a reset of the ACCT module (e.g., via the rst_wrapper AXI interface which controls rst_6) can cause acct_mem to revert to the all-ones state, effectively bypassing any access control restrictions that had been programmed. This contrasts with reglk_wrapper which resets to zero (safe default). The vulnerability is an unsafe reset default combined with an externally-triggerable reset path.
findings.security_impact: After any reset of the ACCT module (including soft resets via rst_wrapper), all access control registers revert to 0xFFFFFFFF, granting unrestricted access to all protected peripherals until software re-programs the access control registers. This creates a window of full access and may be exploitable to permanently bypass access controls if the reset can be triggered at will.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to write to rst_wrapper registers is not fully visible in the provided snippets. However, the unsafe reset default in acct_mem (0xFFFFFFFF) is directly confirmed by source code evidence in SNIP-0005.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper module, which controls register lock values for all peripherals, reads its own reglk_ctrl from an external input (reglk_ctrl_i) but does not apply any lock guard to its own AXI read path. The read-side logic in reglk_wrapper exposes reglk_mem contents via the AXI response without checking the reglk_ctrl bits that govern reglk_wrapper itself, allowing an attacker to read the lock configuration registers through the AXI interface regardless of the lock state. Additionally, acct_wrapper applies reglk_ctrl checks inconsistently across register indices, using only bits [4], [2], [0], and [6] for different register slots with no guard on the default/fallback path.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl as a 16-bit internal signal assigned directly from reglk_ctrl_i input, and reglk_mem as the register bank holding all peripheral lock values. The reglk_ctrl_o output is derived from reglk_mem and broadcast to all other peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_wrapper is the authority for all register lock values; compromise of its read path exposes the entire lock configuration.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with a full AXI interface (reglk_axi_req/reglk_axi_resp), making its internal reglk_mem directly readable via AXI transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: An AXI master can send read requests to reglk_wrapper to retrieve lock register contents.

evidence.description: The axi_lite_interface module provides no permission or lock checking on its own; it simply passes data_i (which is rdata from the wrapper) to axi_resp_o.r.data unconditionally (line 42: assign axi_resp_o.r.data = data_i). There is no reglk_ctrl awareness in this shared interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI interface layer does not enforce any lock-based access restriction; all gating must be done in the wrapper's rdata logic.

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper's read-side logic, registers 0-2 are gated by reglk_ctrl[4], registers 3-5 by reglk_ctrl[2], registers 6-8 by reglk_ctrl[0], and register 9 by reglk_ctrl[6]. The default case returns 32'b0 but there is no guard on the address decode path itself. More critically, the reglk_wrapper module (SNIP-0008) has no analogous read-guard shown in the source snippets - its rdata is produced without any reglk_ctrl check applied to the reglk_mem reads.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock controller's own registers can be read back without a lock check, and acct_wrapper uses inconsistent bit assignments for lock guards across register slots.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper controls reset signals (rst_1 through rst_13) for all major peripherals including AES, SHA256, etc. It receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i but also has an AXI-accessible rdata path. The debug_mode_i input is present but its interaction with the lock mechanism is not shown to be enforced in the read path.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset control registers are sensitive resources accessible via AXI without demonstrated read-path lock enforcement.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper receives debug_mode_i as an input (line 927 area), suggesting special debug behavior, but the read path snippets do not show debug_mode_i being used to restrict or gate AXI read responses.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i may bypass normal restrictions, but no guard is shown in the read data path for rst_wrapper.

findings.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper is the root of the permission chain: it generates reglk_ctrl_o consumed by all other peripherals to lock their registers. However, the source snippets show that reglk_wrapper's own read path (rdata to axi_resp_o) does not appear to apply any self-referential lock check, meaning an AXI master can read reglk_mem contents directly. The shared axi_lite_interface applies no permission checks itself. In acct_wrapper, the read guards are present but inconsistently applied (different reglk_ctrl bits for different register groups with no coverage of all bits). The rst_wrapper controls safety-critical reset signals and receives debug_mode_i, but no gating of reads based on either lock or debug state is shown in the available read-path snippets.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI master could read the register lock configuration from reglk_wrapper, learning which security features are locked and potentially using this information to craft targeted attacks. It could also read acct_wrapper's access control tables for entries not covered by lock bits, and potentially read or influence rst_wrapper's reset control registers, enabling denial-of-service by resetting peripherals or reading sensitive reset state.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-side logic of reglk_wrapper is not included in the source snippets (only signal declarations and AXI interface instantiation are shown), so it is possible that a read guard exists in code not captured here. Similarly, the rst_wrapper read path beyond line 118 is not shown. The finding is based on the absence of guard logic in the available snippets combined with the structural evidence that axi_lite_interface passes data_i unconditionally.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition includes jtag_unlock as a bypass, allowing JTAG to clear all register-lock memory to zero, effectively removing all access controls. Additionally, the acct_wrapper resets acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-access), and the acc_ctrl_o output unconditionally ORs a we_flag into the permission bits, potentially granting access regardless of stored control values.

evidence.description: The reglk_wrapper reset condition is `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`. When jtag_unlock is asserted, the entire reglk_mem is zeroed (set to 0), clearing all register-lock bits. This means asserting JTAG unlock resets all peripheral access controls, bypassing the intended lock protection.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG unlock signal can force reglk_mem to all-zeros, clearing all lock bits and potentially opening all protected registers.

evidence.description: Line 49: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};` The we_flag is unconditionally ORed into the lowest 8 bits of acc_ctrl_o. If we_flag is asserted by any path, it sets access-grant bits to 1 regardless of the stored acct_mem values, bypassing the access control table.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag can override acct_mem permission bits, granting access without proper authorization checks.

evidence.description: On reset (line 83-84), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries, meaning all access control bits start as fully permissive. If reset occurs at any unexpected time (e.g., via rst_6), all access controls are opened to maximum permission.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset of acct_mem grants all permissions, creating a window of full access during and after reset.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper.acct_ctrl_i gates access (en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i), but the internal reglk_ctrl used for read/write guards is sourced from reglk_ctrl_i which can be zeroed via jtag_unlock reset, removing all per-register read/write locks.
evidence.supports_claim: Zeroing reglk_mem via jtag_unlock removes all read and write locks on reglk registers.

evidence.description: The reglk_wrapper read path (lines 112-122) uses reglk_ctrl[0] to gate readback of reglk_mem. If reglk_mem is zeroed by jtag_unlock reset, reglk_ctrl[0]=0 and all reglk_mem contents become readable, leaking the register-lock configuration.
evidence.supports_claim: After jtag_unlock clears reglk_mem, the read-lock bit (reglk_ctrl[0]) is also 0, enabling sensitive readback of all lock register contents.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed as `acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i])`, combining multiple permission bits. This value feeds acct_ctrl_i into downstream wrappers, but the integrity depends on acc_ctrl being correctly initialized and not being overridden by we_flag.
evidence.supports_claim: Permission propagation to all wrappers relies on acc_ctrl integrity, which can be compromised by we_flag override.

findings.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present. First, in reglk_wrapper, the synchronous reset condition includes jtag_unlock as an active-high bypass: asserting jtag_unlock zeroes all reglk_mem entries, clearing all lock bits and removing all read/write protections on register-locked peripherals. Since reglk_ctrl is sourced from reglk_mem, this also removes the read-lock, enabling sensitive readback. Second, in acct_wrapper, the acc_ctrl_o output unconditionally ORs we_flag into the permission bits before driving downstream consumers, meaning any path that asserts we_flag can grant access regardless of the stored acct_mem permission table. The acct_mem also resets to all-ones (fully permissive) on any reset event.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can clear all peripheral register locks by asserting jtag_unlock, then freely read or write all lock-protected registers including cryptographic key registers, reset control, and access-control tables. The we_flag override in acc_ctrl_o can additionally grant access to peripherals without proper authorization. Combined, these allow full bypass of the hardware permission security model.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of we_flag and the conditions under which it is asserted are not shown in the provided snippets, so the full exploitability of the we_flag path cannot be fully confirmed. The jtag_unlock vulnerability is directly evidenced in source code.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper reset condition sets acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), granting maximum access permissions by default on reset, rather than resetting to a secure/deny-all state.

evidence.description: On reset (when ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) is true), acct_mem entries are initialized to 32'hffffffff. Since acct_mem holds access control values that are exported as acc_ctrl_o, a reset event sets all access control bits to 1, which grants full access permissions to all peripherals rather than denying access.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset to all-ones in access control memory means any reset event (including rst_6 assertion) causes all peripherals to become fully accessible, violating the principle of fail-secure.

evidence.description: The acc_ctrl_o output is derived directly from acct_mem: assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. This controls peripheral access permissions system-wide. Resetting acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF means acc_ctrl_o becomes all-ones on reset.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem content directly drives access control outputs, so insecure reset values propagate immediately to permission enforcement.

evidence.description: rst_6 is assigned from rst_mem[6] in rst_wrapper, meaning software-controlled reset signals can trigger the acct_wrapper reset path, causing acct_mem to be set to 0xFFFFFFFF and opening all access gates.
evidence.supports_claim: A software-controlled reset signal (rst_6) can force acct_mem to all-ones, allowing an attacker who can trigger rst_6 to gain full peripheral access.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl (which comes from acc_ctrl_o of acct_wrapper) and is propagated to all peripheral wrappers as the access control input. When acct_mem resets to 0xFFFFFFFF, acc_ctrl becomes all-ones and acc_ctrl_c[i][j] becomes 1 for all peripherals, bypassing all access restrictions.
evidence.supports_claim: The all-ones reset value propagates through acc_ctrl_c to enable access to all peripherals simultaneously.

evidence.description: The en signal is gated by acct_ctrl_i (assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i), which provides per-transaction access control. However, the reset value of acct_mem itself is insecure regardless of this gating, as the stored access control values determine what permissions are granted when en is active.
evidence.supports_claim: The access-enable gating does not compensate for the insecure reset value stored in acct_mem; the content of acct_mem is what determines granted permissions.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module manages access control memory (acct_mem) that governs which privilege levels can access which peripherals. On reset (triggered by either rst_ni deassertion or rst_6 assertion), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff per entry. This is an insecure default because all access control bits being 1 means all permissions are granted. The rst_6 signal is software-controllable via the rst_wrapper module, meaning software can deliberately trigger a reset to obtain maximum access permissions. The insecure reset value propagates through acc_ctrl_o and acc_ctrl_c to all peripheral wrappers in the system.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with write access to the rst_wrapper registers can assert rst_6 to reset acct_mem to all-ones, instantly granting full access to all protected peripherals regardless of their configured lock values. This completely bypasses the access control mechanism for the duration until software reconfigures acct_mem.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of each bit in acct_mem (whether 1 means grant or deny) is inferred from context (acc_ctrl values being ORed with we_flag to produce acc_ctrl_o). The full rst_wrapper register write logic that controls rst_6 is only partially visible. However, the reset-to-all-ones pattern in security-critical access control memory is a clear violation of fail-secure design principles.

findings.summary: The jtag_unlock signal in reglk_wrapper resets all register-lock memory (reglk_mem) to zero, bypassing the normal lock protection and clearing all peripheral access controls without authentication.

evidence.description: The reset condition in reglk_wrapper is: `if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9))`. When jtag_unlock is asserted, the entire condition becomes true and reglk_mem[0..5] are all cleared to 0. This means asserting jtag_unlock alone (without rst_ni being deasserted) clears all register-lock values, effectively unlocking all peripherals simultaneously.
evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock directly triggers a full reset of all lock registers (reglk_mem) to 0x0, bypassing all lock protections without any privilege or authentication check.

evidence.description: reglk_mem stores the lock/unlock state for all peripherals. reglk_ctrl_o is derived directly from reglk_mem and distributed to all peripheral wrappers to gate read/write access. Clearing reglk_mem via jtag_unlock disables all protections across the system.
evidence.supports_claim: The resource being unprotected by the jtag_unlock bypass is the entire register lock state controlling access to all peripherals.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals and its reglk_ctrl_o output feeds the reglk_ctrl signal distributed to all peripheral wrappers (acct_wrapper, rst_wrapper, etc.).
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl signal controls access protections system-wide; clearing it via jtag_unlock removes all protection.

evidence.description: jtag_unlock is declared as a plain logic signal with no privilege gate, authentication check, or secure boot state dependency shown in the source. It is passed directly from riscv_peripherals to reglk_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_unlock signal has no visible guard enforcing that it can only be asserted by an authenticated JTAG session or in an appropriate lifecycle state.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl bits to gate read and write access to acct_mem entries. If reglk_mem is cleared (all bits 0), reglk_ctrl bits that guard these entries are also 0, which in acct_wrapper means writes are allowed (reglk_ctrl[5]=0 permits write) and reads are allowed (reglk_ctrl[4]=0 permits read).
evidence.supports_claim: Clearing reglk_mem via jtag_unlock removes write and read locks on access-control memory, allowing an attacker to modify peripheral access permissions.

findings.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module contains a reset condition that triggers not only on rst_ni deassertion but also on jtag_unlock or rst_9 assertion. When jtag_unlock is asserted, all six reglk_mem registers are cleared to zero. These registers are the lock state for all peripherals; they are output as reglk_ctrl_o and distributed system-wide. Clearing them to zero disables all register-lock protections simultaneously. There is no visible authentication, privilege check, or lifecycle guard on jtag_unlock in the source snippets. This constitutes a bypass of the entire lock protection hierarchy.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with the ability to assert jtag_unlock (e.g., via a JTAG interface without proper authentication enforcement) can instantly clear all peripheral register-lock values, granting full read and write access to all protected peripheral registers including access control tables (acct_mem). This could allow privilege escalation, modification of access control policies, and potential system reset of arbitrary peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source does not show the logic that drives jtag_unlock (e.g., the JTAG DMI authentication module). If jtag_unlock is only asserted after a cryptographic authentication sequence, the risk is reduced. However, the lock register clear on jtag_unlock is architecturally dangerous regardless because it clears to 0 (unlocked) rather than a safe locked state.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper module resets all register-lock memory (reglk_mem) to zero when jtag_unlock is asserted, bypassing the intended lock state and effectively clearing all peripheral protection bits controlled by reglk_ctrl.

evidence.description: The reset condition `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)` causes reglk_mem to be cleared to 0 whenever jtag_unlock is asserted. Since reglk_mem holds the lock bits for all peripherals, this means any entity that can assert jtag_unlock can clear all register locks, bypassing the intended security policy.
evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock assertion resets all reglk_mem entries to 0, which disables all peripheral register locks

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o is directly driven from reglk_mem concatenation. When reglk_mem is zeroed via jtag_unlock, reglk_ctrl_o becomes all zeros, removing all lock protections for NB_PERIPHERALS peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_ctrl_o=0 means all peripheral register locks are deasserted simultaneously

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o from reglk_wrapper is distributed to all peripheral wrappers including acct_wrapper, rst_wrapper and others as their reglk_ctrl_i. Zeroing reglk_mem propagates to all downstream consumers.
evidence.supports_claim: The single reglk_ctrl bus feeds all peripheral lock inputs; clearing it via jtag_unlock removes all access controls system-wide

evidence.description: acct_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[4], etc.) to guard both writes and reads of acct_mem. If reglk_ctrl is 0 (cleared via jtag_unlock path), all these guards evaluate to false and acct_mem becomes fully readable and writable.
evidence.supports_claim: A zeroed reglk_ctrl removes all read and write guards in acct_wrapper, exposing access-control memory to unauthorized modification and readback

evidence.description: jtag_unlock is declared as a standalone logic signal sourced through the JTAG debug interface. Its assertion is a debug/test mechanism that feeds directly into the reglk_wrapper reset condition without requiring any privilege-level check visible in this chain.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG-controlled jtag_unlock can clear all register locks without privilege guard, constituting a debug bypass of the security lock mechanism

findings.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module maintains register-lock bits (reglk_mem) that control read/write access to all peripheral register banks across the SoC. The write-side always block uses a reset condition that includes jtag_unlock: `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`. When jtag_unlock is asserted (1), the entire condition becomes true and reglk_mem is cleared to 0. Because reglk_ctrl_o is combinatorially derived from reglk_mem, this immediately removes all peripheral lock protections. Downstream consumers such as acct_wrapper use individual bits of reglk_ctrl to gate both reads and writes of security-sensitive memory (acct_mem). With all bits zero, both read and write guards are bypassed. This constitutes a privilege bypass: an attacker or test infrastructure that can assert jtag_unlock can unilaterally clear all access controls.

findings.summary: AXI protection field (aw_prot/ar_prot) is hardwired to zero for all security-sensitive peripherals (RST, RSA, RNG, AES0, PLIC), meaning all AXI transactions are issued with unprivileged/unsecure/data-access attributes, bypassing any downstream AXI protection-based access control.

evidence.supports_claim: The PLIC peripheral receives all AXI transactions with aw_prot=0 (unprivileged, secure, data), removing any protection-level distinction on accesses.

evidence.supports_claim: The RSA cryptographic accelerator receives all AXI transactions with zero protection bits, meaning unprivileged software can issue transactions indistinguishable from privileged ones.

evidence.description: The rst_wrapper instantiation shows reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i guard inputs are present, indicating the design intends to enforce access control, but the AXI prot field fed to the wrapper is always zero.
evidence.supports_claim: Despite presence of register-lock and access-control inputs, the AXI prot=0 means any downstream AXI interconnect firewall or monitor relying on protection bits cannot distinguish privileged vs. unprivileged initiators.

evidence.description: The acct_wrapper (Access Control Table) also receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i, but the AXI interface to it is driven from the same bus where prot fields are zeroed out on all peripheral request paths.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control enforcement infrastructure (acct_wrapper, reglk_wrapper) coexists with zero-valued prot fields, meaning any AXI-prot-based security policy is ineffective.

findings.reasoning_summary: Across all security-sensitive peripherals instantiated in riscv_peripherals (PLIC, RST controller, RSA, RNG, AES0), the AXI aw_prot and ar_prot fields are unconditionally assigned to zero. The AXI4 protection encoding (prot[0]=privileged, prot[1]=non-secure, prot[2]=instruction) is the standard mechanism for downstream firewalls and interconnect monitors to enforce privilege-level and security-domain separation. By hardwiring all protection bits to zero (unprivileged, secure, data-access), the design ensures that no AXI-protection-based filtering can distinguish software privilege levels. This is particularly critical for the RST wrapper (system reset control), RSA, RNG, and AES peripherals. The design does include reglk and acct guard infrastructure, but that operates on a separate register-lock mechanism; the AXI-level prot-based access control is entirely absent.
findings.security_impact: Any software running at unprivileged mode (U-mode) that can initiate AXI transactions through the bus fabric will have its requests appear identical to privileged (M-mode/S-mode) requests at all downstream AXI monitors and firewalls, because prot[0] is always 0. This could allow unprivileged code to access cryptographic accelerators (RSA, AES), the hardware RNG entropy source, and the system reset controller without triggering privilege-level-based access denials in AXI interconnect protection checkers.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream AXI interconnect (crossbar/firewall) configuration is not present in the provided snippets, so it is unknown whether any component actually checks prot bits before granting access. If no downstream component enforces AXI protection bits, the zeroing is harmless from a functional perspective. Additionally, the reglk/acct mechanisms may provide sufficient alternative access control. The finding is based purely on the absence of correct prot field population, not on observed unauthorized access.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl (which comes from acc_ctrl_o of acct_wrapper) and is distributed to all peripheral wrappers as access control. Any corruption of acc_ctrl_o propagates to all downstream permission checks.

findings.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities exist in acct_wrapper. First, on any reset event, all acct_mem entries are initialized to 0xFFFFFFFF, meaning all access control bits are set to permissive. Since acc_ctrl_o drives acc_ctrl_c which gates access to every peripheral, all peripherals become accessible to all privilege levels immediately after reset. Second, the acc_ctrl_o assignment unconditionally ORs the lowest 8 bits with {8{we_flag}}, so asserting we_flag forces write-permission bits high regardless of configured policy, bypassing the protection model. Both issues undermine the integrity of the access control chain.
findings.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software that can trigger a reset or assert we_flag gains full access to all protected peripherals including the bootrom, reset controller, and register-lock controller. This allows privilege escalation, arbitrary reset of security-critical IP, modification of register-lock state, and potential compromise of the entire security architecture.

The full acct_wrapper port list for we_flag_0 source is partially visible. However, the OR-bypass in acc_ctrl_o assignment and the all-ones reset initialization are both clearly present in the source snippets.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper resets acct_mem to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF) on reset, granting full access by default, and the acc_ctrl_o output permanently ORs the lowest permission word with {8{we_flag}}, allowing we_flag to unconditionally elevate access-control bits regardless of the lock state.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni or rst_6 asserted), all acct_mem entries are set to 32'hffffffff. This means the access-control memory defaults to fully-permissive on every reset event, rather than a restrictive default.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initialises permission memory to all-ones, making the post-reset state maximally permissive instead of maximally restrictive.

evidence.description: Line 49: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};` — the we_flag input is unconditionally ORed into the lowest access-control word before it is exported. No reglk_ctrl lock bit, no acct_ctrl_i gate, and no privilege check guards this OR operation. Any entity that can assert we_flag can therefore set arbitrary permission bits in acc_ctrl_o.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag bypasses all register-lock and access-control guards to force permission bits high in the exported acc_ctrl_o signal.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived directly from acc_ctrl, which is driven by acc_ctrl_o from acct_wrapper. It is used throughout riscv_peripherals to gate peripheral access (e.g., bootrom, rst_wrapper, reglk_wrapper). Corrupted values here affect all downstream permission checks.
evidence.supports_claim: The corrupted acc_ctrl_o propagates into acc_ctrl_c, which is the system-wide access-control signal used to gate every peripheral.

evidence.description: Line 517: `assign rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0];` — bootrom access is gated by acc_ctrl_c bit 0 for the current privilege level. If we_flag forces bit 0 high via the OR in acc_ctrl_o, unprivileged software gains bootrom access.
evidence.supports_claim: The downstream guard relies on acc_ctrl_c, which can be corrupted by we_flag, undermining the privilege-level check.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper receives `acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12]`. If acc_ctrl_c is manipulated through we_flag, access to the reset controller can be improperly granted.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset-controller access is gated by the same acc_ctrl_c signal that can be forced high by we_flag.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper resets reglk_mem to 0 (all locks cleared) when jtag_unlock is asserted, independently of the normal rst_ni path. This provides an additional vector: JTAG unlock clears all register locks, and with we_flag forcing acc_ctrl bits the attacker can both unlock and grant themselves access.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG unlock zeroes all lock bits in reglk_mem, and we_flag can simultaneously force acc_ctrl bits high, together producing a fully open permission state.

findings.reasoning_summary: Two concrete source-code vulnerabilities exist. First, acct_mem is reset to 0xFFFFFFFF (fully permissive) rather than 0x0 (fully restrictive), meaning any reset event leaves the system in a maximally open permission state until software explicitly tightens the policy. Second, the combinational output acc_ctrl_o permanently ORs we_flag into the lowest access-control word with no lock or privilege guard, so any agent able to assert we_flag can unconditionally set permission bits in the signal that gates every downstream peripheral, including the bootrom and reset controller.
findings.security_impact: An attacker who can assert we_flag (an input to acct_wrapper with no documented privilege requirement in the snippets) can force access-control bits high in acc_ctrl_o, bypassing privilege-level checks for all peripherals that rely on acc_ctrl_c. Combined with the all-ones reset default, the window between reset and software reconfiguration also leaves every peripheral fully accessible. In the worst case this enables unprivileged access to the bootrom, reset controller, and register-lock controller.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of we_flag (who drives it and under what conditions) is not shown in the provided snippets; it is declared as an input to acct_wrapper but its driver in riscv_peripherals is not included. If we_flag is always tied to a privileged-only signal the second vulnerability is partially mitigated, but the all-ones reset default remains unconditionally problematic.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper (Access Control Table) receives AXI requests directly through axi_lite_interface without any privilege or permission guard enforced at the AXI protocol level. The reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs are present at the wrapper port but the AXI request path in axi_lite_interface processes all incoming transactions unconditionally, with no check that blocks or gates writes/reads based on lock or privilege state before accepting and executing them.

evidence.description: The axi_lite_interface state machine (lines 74-134) accepts aw_valid, ar_valid, w_valid, r_ready, and b_ready signals from axi_req_i unconditionally. There is no gating condition referencing reglk_ctrl, acct_ctrl, or any privilege signal before a transaction is accepted, address latched, or write/read enable asserted (en_o, we_o).
evidence.supports_claim: No permission guard is applied inside axi_lite_interface before enabling access to the protected peripheral registers.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper instantiates axi_lite_interface_i and passes axi_req_i directly. The wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i on its ports (SNIP-0004) but the AXI interface sub-module receives no such control signals; there is no combinational gating of axi_req_i fields before they reach axi_lite_interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The acct_axi_req bus is forwarded into axi_lite_interface without being gated by the register-lock or access-control signals available at the wrapper boundary.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i [7:0] and acct_ctrl_i as inputs intended to gate access, but these are only used internally (e.g., for acc_ctrl_o composition via we_flag) and are not wired into the axi_lite_interface instantiation to suppress incoming AXI transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: Permission state signals exist at the wrapper level but are not propagated to the AXI interface to enforce access control on incoming bus transactions.

evidence.description: The ACCT (Access Control Table) block is instantiated in riscv_peripherals and receives acct_axi_req. This block controls access permissions for peripherals across the SoC; unauthorized writes to it could grant or revoke access rights for any peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: The ACCT is a high-value security target; missing guards at its AXI interface allow untrusted bus masters to modify access control settings.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper similarly instantiates axi_lite_interface without forwarding reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i into the AXI sub-module. Writes to the register-lock table via AXI are not gated by the existing lock state, meaning an attacker can overwrite lock values unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-lock peripheral can be written via AXI without any check that the lock itself allows modification, defeating the intended lock-before-boot security model.

evidence.description: acct_axi_req fields are tied off to benign constants (id=0, prot=0, etc.) showing no AxPROT-based privilege encoding is used for the ACCT bus, confirming that bus-level privilege filtering is absent for this security-critical peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: Without AxPROT or any equivalent privilege signal enforced on the ACCT AXI path, any bus master that can route transactions to this address range can read or write the access control table.

findings.reasoning_summary: The axi_lite_interface module, shared by acct_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, and rst_wrapper, processes all AXI transactions unconditionally. Each wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i control inputs, but these are never used to gate the AXI request signals before they are consumed by axi_lite_interface. The state machine in axi_lite_interface asserts en_o and we_o purely based on AXI handshake signals, with no privilege or lock check. This allows any bus master capable of reaching the ACCT or REGLK address aperture to read or modify access-control and register-lock state without restriction, undermining the entire permission architecture of the design.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged or compromised bus master can overwrite the Access Control Table (ACCT) to grant itself access to any peripheral, or overwrite the Register Lock (REGLK) table to unlock previously protected registers, circumventing the hardware-enforced permission model for the entire SoC.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that upstream crossbar or bus-fabric logic filters transactions by privilege level before they reach these wrappers. The source snippets do not include the crossbar or arbitration logic, so bus-fabric-level filtering cannot be ruled out entirely. However, based solely on the wrapper and axi_lite_interface code provided, no such filtering is visible within the permission chain itself.

findings.summary: AXI protection (PROT) field for AES0 peripheral is hardcoded to zero, disabling AXI bus-level privilege and security signaling

evidence.description: Line 1250 sets aes0_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and line 1261 sets aes0_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. The AXI4 PROT field encodes privilege level (bit 0), security state (bit 1), and instruction/data access (bit 2). Forcing both write-channel and read-channel PROT to zero statically means all AES0 transactions are permanently marked as unprivileged, non-secure, data accesses regardless of the actual processor privilege mode or security context.
evidence.supports_claim: Both aw.prot and ar.prot are hardcoded to '0 with no conditioning on privilege level or security state, bypassing AXI bus-level access-control signaling for the AES cryptographic peripheral.
