evidence.description: The aes0_axi_req structure drives the AXI bus interface to the AES0 cryptographic accelerator wrapper. AES is a security-sensitive peripheral handling key material and encrypted data; its bus transactions should carry correct privilege/security attributes.
evidence.supports_claim: AES0 is the protected resource whose bus-level security signaling is neutralized by the hardcoded PROT=0 assignments.

evidence.description: The comment at line 1243 states these are 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite', but aw.prot and ar.prot are architecturally meaningful security fields. Treating them as don't-care tie-offs removes the only bus-level privilege guard on access to the AES peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: No guard logic conditions the PROT field on processor privilege mode or security state; the 'tie-off' justification incorrectly treats security-relevant fields as irrelevant.

findings.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that both the AW-channel protection field (aes0_axi_req.aw.prot) and the AR-channel protection field (aes0_axi_req.ar.prot) are unconditionally assigned '0. In AXI4, the PROT field signals privilege level, secure/non-secure world, and instruction vs. data access. Hardcoding it to zero means the AES0 peripheral can never distinguish privileged from unprivileged accesses or secure from non-secure transactions via bus attributes. Any downstream interconnect or firewall that relies on PROT to enforce access policy for the AES peripheral will always see the same permissive (unprivileged, non-secure) attribute, effectively bypassing AXI bus-level access control for the cryptographic accelerator.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure bus master whose transactions are routed through the same interconnect as the AES0 peripheral could potentially access AES0 registers (keys, plaintext, ciphertext, control) if the interconnect firewall relies on PROT bits to differentiate access rights. Additionally, a secure-world AES usage model is undermined because all AES0 bus transactions appear as non-secure, which could cause a TrustZone-aware interconnect to reject or misroute legitimate secure accesses or allow non-secure masters to satisfy firewall checks.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether a downstream interconnect or firewall actually enforces policy based on PROT bits is not visible in this chain context. If no PROT-aware firewall is present, the practical impact is limited to protocol non-compliance. The AXI-lite wrapper may also internally override PROT before forwarding transactions; that wrapper source is not shown. Confidence is medium rather than high for these reasons.

findings.summary: AXI protection (prot) and lock fields for AES1 AXI requests are permanently tied to zero, disabling AXI access-protection signaling for the AES cryptographic peripheral.

evidence.description: Lines 1336 and 1347 permanently assign aes1_axi_req.aw.prot and aes1_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (unprivileged, non-secure, data access). Lines 1334 and 1345 permanently assign the lock fields to '0, disabling exclusive/locked access semantics. These fields are statically forced and cannot be overridden by any upstream logic, so all AXI transactions to the AES1 peripheral are issued with the least-privileged, non-secure protection attribute regardless of the actual initiator privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI AxPROT[1] bit (bit 1 = non-secure) is hardwired to 0, meaning every transaction to the AES engine is marked as non-secure data access. A properly secured AES peripheral that checks AxPROT to enforce secure-world-only access would be bypassed because the protection attributes never reflect the true security context of the initiating master.

findings.reasoning_summary: The snippet shows that the AXI request structure fields aes1_axi_req.aw.prot and aes1_axi_req.ar.prot are unconditionally assigned '0. In AXI, the AxPROT field encodes privilege level, security domain (secure vs. non-secure), and instruction/data distinction. Tying prot to zero signals unprivileged, non-secure, data transactions for every access to the AES peripheral. If the AES slave or any interconnect firewall uses AxPROT to gate access to cryptographic registers or key material, this hardcoding defeats that check entirely. Similarly, the aw.lock and ar.lock fields are tied off to zero, preventing any exclusive-access or atomic-update semantics that might be used for key-loading operations.
findings.security_impact: All AXI transactions to the AES1 cryptographic engine are permanently marked as non-secure and unprivileged. Any TrustZone-aware interconnect or peripheral protection unit that relies on AxPROT to restrict AES key/config register access to the secure world will be rendered ineffective. An unprivileged or non-secure master that reaches this bus path could access the AES engine without triggering access-control violations, potentially exposing key material or allowing unauthorized cryptographic operations.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether the downstream AES slave or interconnect actually checks AxPROT is not visible in this chain context. If no downstream component enforces AxPROT-based filtering, the practical exploitability is reduced. Additionally, the full data-path from software to this AXI request structure (i.e., whether an unprivileged initiator can reach this path) is not shown here.

findings.summary: AXI protection (prot) fields for AES2 AXI requests are permanently tied to zero, disabling privilege, security, and instruction/data access type signaling on the bus.

evidence.description: Lines 1421 and 1432 permanently assign aes2_axi_req.aw.prot and aes2_axi_req.ar.prot to 3'b000 (all zeros). The AXI ARPROT/AWPROT field encodes three bits: [0] privileged access, [1] non-secure access, [2] instruction access. Tying these to zero forces all AES2 transactions to appear as unprivileged, secure, data-type accesses with no runtime enforcement or configurability. Additionally, aes2_axi_req.aw.lock and aes2_axi_req.ar.lock are tied to zero, permanently disabling exclusive access signaling.
evidence.supports_claim: The prot field being hardwired to 0 means downstream AXI slaves relying on prot-based access control (privilege or security domain checks) will always see the AES2 master as making unprivileged, secure-domain, data accesses, regardless of the actual privilege level of the initiating software. This removes any bus-level privilege or security domain separation for AES2 memory transactions.

findings.reasoning_summary: The source snippet at lines 1421 and 1432 shows that both the write-channel (aw.prot) and read-channel (ar.prot) protection fields of the aes2_axi_req AXI request bundle are permanently driven to zero via continuous assigns. The AXI specification uses the prot field to communicate the privilege level, security level, and access type of a transaction to downstream interconnect and slave components. Forcing this field to zero unconditionally means no downstream slave can distinguish an AES2-initiated transaction from a privileged or non-secure access based on the prot signal alone. If any interconnect or peripheral uses prot to gate access, those checks are effectively bypassed for all AES2 traffic.
findings.security_impact: Any AXI slave or interconnect firewall using the protection (prot) field to enforce privilege separation or secure/non-secure domain isolation will grant AES2 transactions the same access as unprivileged, secure, data accesses regardless of actual caller privilege. This could allow unprivileged software to perform cryptographic operations through AES2 that should be restricted to higher privilege levels, or could allow AES2 to access regions that should be blocked based on security domain. The impact is a missing access-control guard on a cryptographic hardware block's bus transactions.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed from the provided snippets alone whether downstream slaves or interconnect actually check the prot field for access control decisions. If no slave enforces prot-based checks, the practical security impact is reduced. The full AXI interconnect and slave configuration would be needed to confirm exploitability. Additionally, whether this is by design (AXI-lite simplification) or a true oversight is unclear from this context alone.

findings.summary: SimDTM debug module is instantiated unconditionally in RTL, allowing debug access (DMI requests) without any privilege or lifecycle guard

evidence.description: SimDTM (SiFive's simulation Debug Transport Module using DPI calls) is instantiated at lines 256-264. The instance directly drives debug_req_valid, debug_req.addr, debug_req_bits_op, and debug_req.data with no surrounding ifdef, parameter gate, lifecycle check, or privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: A simulation-only debug module is present in synthesizable RTL without any conditional compilation or runtime guard.

evidence.description: Line 254: `assign dmi_req.op = dm::dtm_op_t'(debug_req_bits_op);` directly wires the SimDTM output into the DMI request bus. The cast from the raw 2-bit SimDTM output to a privileged debug opcode type is unconditional and unguarded, meaning any value driven by the simulation DPI harness propagates immediately into the debug module interface.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no lock, authentication, or lifecycle check between the SimDTM DPI source and the DMI request that reaches the RISC-V debug module.

evidence.description: The SimDTM instance (lines 256-264) receives clk and reset but the reset polarity (~rst_ni) means once rst_ni is asserted high (normal operation), SimDTM is active. debug_req_valid and all address/data/op fields are driven directly from SimDTM to the debug subsystem with no interposing guard logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug entry path through SimDTM is always active during normal operation.

findings.reasoning_summary: The SimDTM module is a simulation-only DPI-based debug transport that is intended purely for testbench use. Its unconditional instantiation in the synthesizable RTL file riscv_peripherals.sv, and the direct, unguarded assignment of its outputs to the DMI request bus (dmi_req.op, debug_req.addr, debug_req.data), means there is no privilege check, lifecycle lock, or compile-time guard preventing debug commands from reaching the RISC-V debug module. In a synthesized device this represents a permanently open debug backdoor; in simulation it allows unrestricted DMI operations without any authentication.
findings.security_impact: An attacker or unintended simulation agent can issue arbitrary DMI read/write operations to the RISC-V debug module (e.g., halt CPU, access registers, inject code) without any authentication or lifecycle restriction, bypassing all normal privilege controls.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown whether a higher-level wrapper conditionally excludes this module via a parameter or ifdef before synthesis. If such a guard exists at a higher hierarchy level not present in this chain, the severity would be limited to simulation environments only. No such guard is visible in the provided snippets.

findings.summary: DMA AXI request protection field (aw.prot and ar.prot) is hardwired to zero, disabling AXI AxPROT privilege, security, and instruction-type signaling for all DMA transactions.

evidence.description: Line 1963 assigns dma_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and line 1974 assigns dma_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. The AXI4 AxPROT field encodes three bits: [0] privilege level (unprivileged vs. privileged), [1] security level (secure vs. non-secure), and [2] data/instruction access. Tying both to zero permanently marks every DMA read and write as unprivileged, secure, and data-access regardless of the actual privilege context or destination memory region. This means any downstream interconnect or memory protection unit that relies on AxPROT to enforce access controls will see all DMA traffic as uniformly unprivileged-secure, potentially allowing the DMA engine to access secure or privileged memory regions with incorrect protection attributes, or preventing legitimate privileged-secure accesses from being correctly identified.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields of the DMA AXI request are unconditionally forced to zero, bypassing AXI protection signaling and preventing downstream bus protection mechanisms from enforcing privilege and security policies on DMA transactions.

findings.reasoning_summary: The source snippet explicitly shows that both the write-channel protection field (dma_axi_req.aw.prot) and the read-channel protection field (dma_axi_req.ar.prot) are tied to the constant value '0 via continuous assignments labeled as 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite'. In AXI4, AxPROT[0] distinguishes privileged from unprivileged access, AxPROT[1] distinguishes secure from non-secure access, and AxPROT[2] distinguishes instruction from data access. Hard-coding these to zero means all DMA AXI transactions are always presented as unprivileged, secure, and data-type to any downstream interconnect component, memory controller, or bus firewall. Any protection unit that uses AxPROT to gate access (e.g., TrustZone-aware interconnects, MPUs keyed on AXI protection attributes) will not receive accurate privilege or security context for DMA traffic, constituting a missing guard on the DMA access path.
findings.security_impact: A DMA engine operating with hardwired AxPROT=0 (unprivileged, secure, data) can issue AXI transactions that misrepresent the actual privilege level. Downstream interconnect firewalls or memory protection units that rely on AxPROT[0] to restrict privileged-only regions may either incorrectly grant or deny access. More critically, if the DMA is supposed to operate in privileged mode to access privileged memory, the forced-unprivileged encoding may cause it to be denied. Conversely, if a bus firewall uses AxPROT[1]=0 (secure) to allow access to secure memory, the DMA could reach secure regions without proper authorization checks, enabling potential unauthorized data exfiltration or corruption of secure memory.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual impact depends on whether any downstream interconnect component in this SoC enforces access control based on AxPROT bits. If no downstream component checks AxPROT, the practical impact is limited. The chain context does not include the downstream interconnect, memory controller, or bus firewall configuration, so the severity cannot be fully confirmed from this snippet alone. Additionally, the comment 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite' suggests this may be intentional for AXI-lite bridging, but AXI-lite does not define AxPROT as unused; it is still a valid signaling field in AXI-lite.

findings.summary: HMAC AXI request channel has AXI protection bits (aw.prot and ar.prot) permanently tied to zero, disabling privilege/security/instruction access type signaling to the HMAC peripheral.

evidence.description: Line 1591 assigns hmac_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and line 1602 assigns hmac_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. The AXI4 PROT field encodes privilege level (bit 0), security state (bit 1), and instruction/data access (bit 2). Hardcoding this to zero unconditionally signals all HMAC transactions as unprivileged, non-secure, data accesses regardless of the true initiator privilege or security state. This means a transaction from an unprivileged, non-secure context is indistinguishable from one issued by a privileged secure context, removing any downstream access-control enforcement that relies on AxPROT.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields of hmac_axi_req are permanently forced to 0b000, bypassing AXI protection signaling for all HMAC transactions.

evidence.description: The HMAC peripheral is a cryptographic accelerator accessed via AXI. Its registers and key material should only be reachable from privileged, secure software. AXI AxPROT bits are the standard mechanism for communicating this access classification to interconnect firewalls and peripheral access controllers.
evidence.supports_claim: HMAC is a security-sensitive peripheral whose AXI transactions should carry correct protection attributes.

evidence.description: No conditional logic or privilege-level signal is observed feeding into the aw.prot or ar.prot assignments. All twenty continuous assignments are unconditional tie-offs described as 'signals not used by AXI-lite', but AxPROT is a security-relevant field that should reflect the initiator's privilege state.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard or privilege-aware assignment for the protection bits; they are statically zeroed.

findings.reasoning_summary: The source snippet explicitly ties hmac_axi_req.aw.prot and hmac_axi_req.ar.prot to zero (lines 1591 and 1602) as part of a block that 'ties off signals not used by AXI-lite'. The AXI4 specification defines AxPROT[1] as the security bit (0=secure, 1=non-secure) and AxPROT[0] as the privilege bit. Forcing these to zero makes every HMAC transaction appear as a privileged, secure access from the interconnect's perspective, regardless of the true security state of the initiating software. This prevents any TrustZone-aware or privilege-aware firewall/interconnect component from correctly enforcing access control on the HMAC peripheral, and conversely could allow non-secure software that somehow obtains AXI access to appear as a secure initiator.
findings.security_impact: Any downstream AXI interconnect, memory protection unit, or firewall that uses AxPROT to gate access to the HMAC peripheral will always see secure+privileged+data on HMAC transactions. Non-secure or unprivileged software whose AXI requests are forwarded through this path will have their security context stripped, allowing them to appear trusted to the HMAC peripheral and any intermediate security monitors. This could permit unauthorized access to HMAC key material or computation results.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain context does not include evidence of a downstream firewall or interconnect that actually enforces AxPROT-based access control for the HMAC peripheral; if no such enforcement exists the impact is lower. Additionally, in a pure AXI-lite bridge scenario the bridge itself may enforce privilege checks before issuing requests, making the zeroed prot field less critical. The AXI-lite adapter instantiation and its privilege handling logic are not visible in the provided snippets.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition uses jtag_unlock as a reset trigger that clears all register-lock memory (reglk_mem) to zero, which effectively unlocks all peripheral register protections when JTAG asserts jtag_unlock. This means an attacker with JTAG access can bypass all register-lock protections by asserting jtag_unlock, resetting the lock state to unlocked (all zeros).

evidence.description: The always block at line 80 resets reglk_mem to 0 when jtag_unlock is asserted: `if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9))`. When jtag_unlock=1, the condition evaluates to true and all reglk_mem entries are cleared to zero. Since reglk_mem drives the lock bits for all peripherals, asserting jtag_unlock clears all lock protections.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG unlock signal directly resets the register lock memory to all-zeros, bypassing all configured lock protections.

evidence.description: reglk_mem holds the register lock values (6 x 32-bit entries) that are exported as reglk_ctrl_o to all peripherals. When cleared to zero, all lock bits become 0, allowing unrestricted read and write access to all protected peripheral registers.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem is the central permission state for all peripheral register locks; clearing it disables all protections.

evidence.description: Write guard at lines 89-99: `reglk_ctrl[3] ? reglk_mem[0] : wdata` and `reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[N] : wdata`. Read guard at lines 112-122: `reglk_ctrl[0] ? 'b0 : reglk_mem[N]`. Both guards rely on reglk_ctrl bits derived from reglk_ctrl_i (the previous lock value), but when reglk_mem is all zeros after jtag_unlock, reglk_ctrl_o becomes all zeros, and downstream guards also become ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: Guard logic depends on reglk_mem being non-zero to enforce locks; jtag_unlock resets these to zero, defeating the guards.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates reglk_wrapper and connects jtag_unlock directly from the top-level signal to reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock, confirming JTAG can control this input.
evidence.supports_claim: jtag_unlock is a live top-level input wired directly into the register lock controller without additional privilege verification.

evidence.description: jtag_unlock is declared as a plain logic signal in riscv_peripherals at line 239, with no additional authentication or privilege gating shown before it is forwarded to reglk_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_unlock signal is an unauthenticated debug/JTAG control line that directly controls security-critical lock state.

findings.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module uses jtag_unlock as part of a reset condition that clears reglk_mem to zero. Since reglk_mem contains all register-lock bits exported to peripheral wrappers (reglk_ctrl_o), asserting jtag_unlock effectively unlocks all peripheral register protections simultaneously. The source code at line 80 of reglk_wrapper.sv shows the combined reset condition `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`, which triggers clearing of all lock memory when any of rst_ni deasserts, jtag_unlock asserts, or rst_9 asserts. This is a security-critical flaw because JTAG should not be able to clear lock state that is meant to persist as a security boundary even during debug sessions.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical JTAG access can assert jtag_unlock to clear all register-lock protections (reglk_mem becomes all zeros), granting unrestricted read and write access to all peripherals that rely on reglk_ctrl for protection, including cryptographic key registers, access control tables, and reset control. This completely bypasses the intended hardware security model.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact mechanism by which jtag_unlock is driven (i.e., what authentication, if any, the JTAG debug module requires before asserting jtag_unlock) is not visible in the provided snippets. If the JTAG module requires strong authentication before asserting jtag_unlock, the practical exploitability depends on that authentication strength. However, the register-lock clearing behavior on jtag_unlock is unambiguously present in the source code.

findings.summary: AXI protection (prot) field hardwired to zero, disabling privilege, security, and instruction/data access type signaling on peripheral bus transactions

evidence.description: pkt_axi_req.aw.prot and pkt_axi_req.ar.prot are both unconditionally assigned '0 (line 1684 and 1695). The AXI4 ARPROT/AWPROT field encodes three bits: privileged access, secure/non-secure access, and instruction/data access. Forcing all three bits to zero signals unprivileged, secure, data access for every transaction, regardless of the actual privilege level or security state of the originating RISC-V core.
evidence.supports_claim: Both the write-channel (aw.prot='0) and read-channel (ar.prot='0) protection fields are hardwired to zero with no connection to the CPU privilege or security context, meaning downstream interconnect or memory protection units that rely on AxPROT to enforce access control will always see the same fixed value and cannot distinguish between privileged/unprivileged or secure/non-secure initiators.

evidence.description: The pkt_axi_req bus drives accesses to peripherals including fuse_mem (lines 1699-1707), which stores security-critical data (jtag_hash, okey_hash, ikey_hash). Any protection enforced by AXI subordinates based on AxPROT will be ineffective because all transactions appear identical regardless of initiator privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: Security-sensitive fuse memory and other peripherals connected via pkt_axi_req cannot rely on the AXI AxPROT field to enforce privilege-level separation because that field is statically zeroed.

findings.reasoning_summary: The AXI4 specification defines AxPROT[2:0] to carry privilege level (bit 0), secure/non-secure (bit 1), and instruction/data (bit 2) attributes. The code unconditionally ties both aw.prot and ar.prot to all-zeros as part of an AXI-lite adaptation shim. While AXI-lite does not mandate use of AxPROT, if downstream subordinates (e.g., the fuse memory or other security-sensitive peripherals reachable via this bus) implement any access control based on these bits, the static zero value means all requests will always match a single protection profile, bypassing any privilege-based filtering. Specifically, bit 1 being zero signals 'secure access' to any TrustZone-aware or security-zone-aware subordinate, potentially granting untrusted software access to secure resources.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure software initiator could gain access to secure or privileged peripherals (including fuse memory containing key hashes) if those peripherals use AxPROT filtering, because every transaction unconditionally presents as unprivileged+secure+data (prot=3'b000). This breaks privilege separation enforced at the interconnect or memory protection controller level.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain context does not include the subordinate interfaces or memory protection unit logic that would confirm whether AxPROT is actually checked downstream. The vulnerability is confirmed only if at least one subordinate on this bus enforces AxPROT-based access control. Additionally, the comment 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite' suggests intentional design, but the security consequences of static prot=0 are not addressed.

findings.summary: AXI AxPROT field hardwired to zero for all security-sensitive peripherals (RST, RSA, RNG, AES0, PLIC), removing privilege-level and secure/non-secure transaction tagging from bus requests

evidence.description: plic_master AXI-lite tie-offs assign aw_prot = '0 and ar_prot = '0 unconditionally, erasing any privilege or secure/non-secure indication on all PLIC transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: The PLIC master bus permanently presents unprivileged, non-secure data transactions regardless of the actual privilege level of the initiating core.

evidence.description: rst_axi_req.aw.prot and rst_axi_req.ar.prot are both hardwired to '0 (lines 992, 1003). The RST wrapper instantiation at SNIP-0003 accepts reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl inputs, implying access-control logic exists, but the AXI PROT field that downstream interconnects and decoders rely on to enforce privilege separation is permanently cleared.
evidence.supports_claim: Access-control metadata carried by AxPROT is suppressed before it reaches the interconnect, so privilege-level enforcement at the bus level cannot function for RST peripheral accesses.

evidence.description: rsa_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot = '0 (lines 1077, 1088). RSA is a cryptographic accelerator; all its bus transactions are tagged as unprivileged non-secure, allowing any bus master that can reach the interconnect to appear equivalent to a privileged secure access.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI protection attributes are stripped from RSA peripheral transactions, bypassing any interconnect-level privilege filtering.

evidence.description: rng_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and rng_axi_req.ar.prot = '0 (lines 1163, 1174). RNG entropy output can be read without any privilege indication on the bus.
evidence.supports_claim: Privilege separation for RNG reads is not enforced at the AXI transport layer because AxPROT is hardcoded to zero.

evidence.description: aes0_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and aes0_axi_req.ar.prot = '0 (lines 1250, 1261). AES key material and cipher operations are exposed without privilege tagging.
evidence.supports_claim: All AES0 transactions present as non-privileged non-secure on the AXI bus, defeating any downstream protection policy that relies on AxPROT to gate access to cryptographic registers.

evidence.description: The rst_wrapper instantiation passes reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i, showing that register-lock and account-control guards exist at the peripheral level. However, the AXI AxPROT field is still zeroed out (SNIP-0004), meaning the bus-level guard is absent.
evidence.supports_claim: Peripheral-internal guards are present but the AXI-level privilege indication needed for interconnect-layer enforcement is permanently suppressed.

findings.reasoning_summary: Across all security-sensitive peripherals instantiated in riscv_peripherals (PLIC, RST, RSA, RNG, AES0), the AXI AxPROT[2:0] field is unconditionally tied to 3'b000 (unprivileged, non-secure, data access). The AXI specification uses AxPROT to convey privilege level, secure/non-secure world, and instruction/data type so that interconnects, firewalls, and peripheral decoders can enforce access policies. Hardwiring it to zero means (1) no downstream component can distinguish a Machine-mode secure request from a User-mode non-secure one, (2) secure-world isolation mechanisms that key off AxPROT[1] are rendered ineffective, and (3) privilege escalation through these peripherals cannot be detected or blocked at the interconnect level. While some wrappers (e.g., rst_wrapper) accept register-lock and account-control signals internally, the absence of correct AxPROT tagging undermines any policy enforced by external bus infrastructure.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with any bus access can interact with RSA, AES, and RNG peripherals using transactions that appear identical to privileged secure accesses, bypassing interconnect-level privilege filtering. Cryptographic key material in AES registers and RNG entropy can be read or written without the bus indicating the required privilege level, and the RST peripheral controlling system reset can be accessed without privilege indication, potentially enabling unauthorized system reset or privilege escalation.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual interconnect or firewall configuration is not shown; if downstream components do not use AxPROT for access control, the practical impact is reduced. However, permanently clearing a security-relevant bus field is itself a violation of the AXI protection model regardless of current interconnect configuration, and the presence of explicit access-control inputs on at least one wrapper (rst_wrapper) confirms that a protection model is intended.

findings.summary: Commented-out access control check for bootrom access reveals an earlier version that bypassed privilege-level gating; the current active line enforces the check, but the commented line immediately above it indicates the guard was intentionally disabled at some point and could be trivially re-disabled, exposing the bootrom to unprivileged access.

evidence.description: Line 516 contains a commented-out assignment `//assign rom_req = rom_req_acct;` that completely removes the privilege-level and access-control check (`acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]`). The active line 517 does enforce the check, but the commented line demonstrates the guard can be trivially removed. More critically, this shows the design pattern allows bootrom requests to proceed with no privilege or access-control filtering if the comment is removed.
evidence.supports_claim: The presence of the bypass comment directly adjacent to the enforcing assignment indicates an intentional escape path where the access-control guard (`acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]`) on bootrom requests can be and has been disabled, constituting a missing/bypassable guard on the protected bootrom resource.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed as `acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i])`, meaning the access control bit for the bootrom (index 0) is derived from the acct_wrapper output. If this value is wrong or all-ones by default, the guard on line 517 may always pass.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control state feeding the bootrom guard is computed combinatorially from acct_mem, which is initialized and written through the ACCT peripheral, establishing the permission state chain.

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, the enable signal is `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i`, gating ACCT register writes behind the access control input. However, `acc_ctrl_o` is assigned combinatorially as `{acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}`, where `we_flag` can force the lower 8 bits of acc_ctrl_o to all-ones regardless of acct_mem contents, potentially granting all access.
evidence.supports_claim: The `we_flag` input ORed into acc_ctrl_o can override stored access control values, weakening the permission guard that ultimately protects the bootrom and other peripherals.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper receives `acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12]`, tying the reset controller's access permission directly to the current privilege level index into the access control matrix.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege level signal priv_lvl_i directly indexes acc_ctrl_c to produce the permission bit fed into security-critical peripherals including the reset controller.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper uses `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i` to gate register access. The reset controller manages system-wide resets (rst_1 through rst_13) for all security peripherals. If acct_ctrl_i is incorrectly set or bypassed, unprivileged code could trigger resets of security modules.
evidence.supports_claim: Access to the reset controller, which can reset AES, SHA, HMAC, and other security peripherals, is gated only by the acct_ctrl_i bit derived from the potentially-bypassable access control chain.

findings.reasoning_summary: The chain shows that `priv_lvl_i` indexes into `acc_ctrl_c` to produce per-peripheral access control bits. These bits gate enable signals in rst_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, and acct_wrapper, and also guard bootrom requests. Two concrete vulnerabilities are present: (1) The commented-out line 516 (`assign rom_req = rom_req_acct`) shows the bootrom access-control guard can be trivially removed, and represents a known bypass path. (2) In acct_wrapper, the `we_flag` input is ORed into `acc_ctrl_o`, which can unconditionally grant access to all peripherals regardless of the stored acct_mem values, undermining the entire permission chain. The rst_wrapper controls resets for all security peripherals and is protected only by this same chain.
findings.security_impact: An attacker who can manipulate `we_flag` in acct_wrapper, or who operates when the bootrom guard is disabled (as shown by the commented line), can: (1) gain read access to the bootrom regardless of privilege level; (2) override access control values for all 14 peripherals to grant universal access; (3) trigger resets of security-critical peripherals (AES, SHA256, HMAC) via the rst_wrapper if access control is circumvented. This undermines the hardware-enforced privilege separation for all protected peripherals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of `we_flag` and how it is driven are not shown in the provided snippets; it is possible it is only asserted in safe conditions. The commented-out line 516 is not active code but demonstrates a known bypass pattern. Full acct_wrapper write-side logic and the source of we_flag would be needed for higher confidence.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper (register lock controller) receives AXI requests directly without any privilege or lock check at the AXI interface level. The reglk_axi_req signal is wired directly to the axi_lite_interface inside reglk_wrapper, meaning any bus master that can reach this peripheral address range can read or write the register-lock control registers (reglk_ctrl_o) without the AXI interface itself enforcing access control. Similarly, the AXI prot field is hardwired to zero in the tie-off assignments, stripping privilege signaling.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper is instantiated and its reglk_ctrl_o output drives the register-lock control signals for all peripherals. Any write to this module's AXI space changes the lock state of all downstream peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_wrapper controls security-critical lock registers for all peripherals in the system.

evidence.description: Line 1885: assign reglk_axi_req.aw.prot = '0; and line 1885: assign reglk_axi_req.ar.prot = '0; — the AXI protection bits (prot) are unconditionally tied to zero for all transactions to the reglk_wrapper. This removes the AXI privilege/secure/instruction attribute signaling, meaning the reglk_wrapper's AXI interface cannot distinguish privileged from unprivileged or secure from non-secure transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI prot field forced to zero strips privilege-level signaling from all transactions to the register-lock controller.

evidence.description: The reglk_wrapper passes axi_req_i directly into axi_lite_interface_i without any internal guard checking privilege level, lock state, or other access-control qualifier before allowing reads or writes to reglk_mem.
evidence.supports_claim: No access-control guard is applied inside reglk_wrapper before dispatching AXI transactions to the register-lock memory.

evidence.description: The axi_lite_interface state machine accepts aw_valid and ar_valid unconditionally without checking any privilege, authentication, or protection attribute. It will respond to any valid AXI request and assert en_o/we_o to the register file.
evidence.supports_claim: axi_lite_interface performs no permission check; any requester reaching this module can read or write the backing register file.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper has reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, and jtag_unlock inputs indicating the intent to gate access. However, these signals are used for internal logic but the AXI interface path shown (axi_lite_interface_i instantiation) does not gate the AXI transactions based on these inputs at the bus-interface level.
evidence.supports_claim: Lock-control inputs exist but are not enforced on the AXI request path into the register-lock memory.

findings.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper is the system-level register-lock controller whose outputs (reglk_ctrl_o) govern whether other peripherals can be accessed. The AXI interface wired to it (axi_lite_interface) has no privilege or authentication check and unconditionally services any read or write request. Furthermore, the AXI prot bits for reglk_axi_req are hardwired to zero, destroying any upstream privilege information. A bus master with access to the reglk_wrapper's address range can overwrite the lock registers, unlocking all peripherals, or read back the current lock state, without any hardware-enforced access control at the AXI level.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious bus master that can address the reglk_wrapper can overwrite the register-lock control values, effectively disabling all register-lock protections for every peripheral in the system. This is a complete bypass of the security mechanism the reglk_wrapper is designed to enforce.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show the full internal body of reglk_wrapper (e.g., whether reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, or jtag_unlock are used to gate write operations to reglk_mem after the AXI decoding stage). If those signals do gate writes in the write-enable path not captured here, the vulnerability severity would be reduced. Additionally, upstream address-decode or firewall logic outside this chain might restrict who can reach the reglk_wrapper address range.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper resets all register-lock values (reglk_mem) to zero when jtag_unlock is asserted, clearing all peripheral protection bits and allowing unrestricted access to all locked registers.

evidence.description: The reset condition in reglk_wrapper is: `if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9))`. This means that asserting jtag_unlock forces all reglk_mem entries to 0x0, clearing all register lock bits across all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting jtag_unlock bypasses the register lock protection by zeroing reglk_mem, which propagates as reglk_ctrl_o = 0 to all consumers.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o is directly assigned from reglk_mem: `assign reglk_ctrl_o = {reglk_mem[5], reglk_mem[4], reglk_mem[3], reglk_mem[2], reglk_mem[1], reglk_mem[0]};`. When reglk_mem is zeroed by jtag_unlock, reglk_ctrl_o becomes all zeros.
evidence.supports_claim: When reglk_mem is cleared, the entire reglk_ctrl bus distributed to all peripherals becomes zero, disabling all lock bits.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o from reglk_wrapper feeds directly into riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl, which is then distributed to acct_wrapper, rst_wrapper, and reglk_wrapper itself as reglk_ctrl_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The zeroed reglk_ctrl propagates to all peripheral wrappers, removing all read and write protections simultaneously.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl bits to guard writes (e.g., `acct_mem[00] <= reglk_ctrl[5] ? acct_mem[00] : wdata`) and reads (e.g., `rdata = reglk_ctrl[4] ? 'b0 : acct_mem[0]`). When reglk_ctrl is all zeros, all guards evaluate to false, enabling full read and write access.
evidence.supports_claim: With reglk_ctrl=0, all write-lock and read-lock guards in acct_wrapper are bypassed, exposing all acct_mem entries to unrestricted access.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper read side uses `rdata = reglk_ctrl[0] ? 'b0 : reglk_mem[n]` for all entries. With reglk_ctrl[0]=0 (after jtag_unlock clears memory), all reglk_mem values become readable.
evidence.supports_claim: All register lock configuration values become readable when the lock is cleared via jtag_unlock.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i from reglk_ctrl[12*8+7:12*8], which is also zeroed when jtag_unlock fires, disabling any write/read protection in rst_wrapper as well.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset controller's register protections are also bypassed when jtag_unlock clears reglk_mem.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper controls peripheral reset lines (rst_1 through rst_13 assigned from rst_mem). Combined with the cleared register locks, an attacker with jtag_unlock access can both read/write protected registers and trigger resets of security-critical peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The impact extends beyond register lock bypass to include potential unauthorized reset of security peripherals.

findings.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper implements a register lock mechanism protecting multiple peripherals. The reset condition `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)` causes all reglk_mem entries to be zeroed whenever jtag_unlock is asserted. Since reglk_ctrl_o is derived directly from reglk_mem and is distributed as reglk_ctrl to all peripheral wrappers (acct_wrapper, rst_wrapper, reglk_wrapper itself), asserting jtag_unlock effectively disables all register lock protections simultaneously. This allows an attacker who can assert jtag_unlock to read and write all previously locked registers in all peripherals. The reglk_ctrl[0]=0 condition also makes the reglk_mem itself readable through the read-side guard in reglk_wrapper, exposing the lock configuration. There is no privilege check or authentication requirement shown in the source snippets before jtag_unlock takes effect.
findings.security_impact: An attacker who can assert the jtag_unlock signal gains the ability to bypass all register lock protections across all peripherals in the design simultaneously. This includes the ability to overwrite access control registers (acct_mem), read sensitive register lock configuration, and potentially trigger unauthorized resets of security peripherals via rst_wrapper. The jtag_unlock mechanism functions as a master key that can defeat the entire register locking security architecture with no apparent privilege escalation requirement visible in the RTL.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of the jtag_unlock signal and any upstream authentication or privilege check controlling it are not present in the provided snippets. It is possible that jtag_unlock is gated by a JTAG authentication mechanism outside this chain context. However, the RTL behavior as shown is that a single signal unconditionally clears all security locks with no visible guard in the reglk_wrapper itself.

findings.summary: Boot ROM selection mux is broken: both branches of the ternary operator assign rom_rdata_linux, making ariane_boot_sel_i ineffective and always booting into Linux mode regardless of the boot selector input.

evidence.description: Line 534 reads: `assign rom_rdata = (ariane_boot_sel_i) ? rom_rdata_linux : rom_rdata_linux;` — both the true and false branches of the conditional assignment reference `rom_rdata_linux`. The comment states the intent is to support baremetal mode when using pitonstream, implying `rom_rdata_bm` should be used in one branch, but it is never selected. The boot selector input `ariane_boot_sel_i` has no effect.
evidence.supports_claim: The mux intended to switch between baremetal and Linux boot ROMs is permanently stuck on the Linux ROM, bypassing the intended boot mode control.

evidence.description: Three ROM data signals are declared: `rom_rdata`, `rom_rdata_bm`, and `rom_rdata_linux`. The existence of `rom_rdata_bm` confirms a baremetal boot ROM path exists in the design, but it is never routed to `rom_rdata` due to the copy-paste error in the mux assignment.
evidence.supports_claim: The baremetal boot ROM (`rom_rdata_bm`) is a distinct protected resource that should be selectable via `ariane_boot_sel_i` but is permanently excluded from the output mux.

findings.reasoning_summary: The continuous assignment on line 534 uses `ariane_boot_sel_i` as a selector but assigns `rom_rdata_linux` in both branches of the ternary. The declared signal `rom_rdata_bm` (visible in SNIP-0001) is never driven to `rom_rdata`. This is a classic copy-paste bug that defeats the boot mode selection logic entirely. The system will always boot from the Linux ROM image regardless of the hardware boot selector pin state, preventing the intended baremetal boot path from ever being used.
findings.security_impact: An operator or attacker who sets the boot selector to request baremetal mode will still receive the Linux boot ROM image. This bypasses the intended boot mode access control, potentially allowing execution of an unintended firmware image. Conversely, it may prevent legitimate baremetal secure boot paths from being exercised, undermining boot integrity guarantees. If the baremetal ROM contains a more restricted or verified boot sequence, its bypass is a security regression.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet does not show the instantiation or content of the baremetal ROM (`rom_rdata_bm`), so the exact security consequence of always using `rom_rdata_linux` depends on the threat model. However, the code defect itself is unambiguous from the source snippet.

findings.summary: AXI protection (prot) fields for RSA peripheral AXI requests are hard-coded to zero, disabling privilege, security, and instruction/data access type signaling on the bus.

evidence.description: Both rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot are statically tied to '0 (line 1077 and 1088). AXI prot[1] encodes privileged vs. unprivileged access, prot[1] encodes secure vs. non-secure, and prot[2] encodes instruction vs. data. Setting all bits to zero means every RSA AXI transaction claims unprivileged, secure, data access regardless of the actual caller context; downstream bus protection monitors or memory protection units that rely on these fields cannot enforce correct privilege or security-domain separation for RSA operations.
evidence.supports_claim: The prot field is unconditionally zero, preventing any downstream firewall or memory protection unit from enforcing privilege or Secure/Non-Secure access control on RSA AXI transactions.

evidence.description: The rsa_axi_req structure drives AXI transactions to the RSA accelerator peripheral, which handles cryptographic key material. Correct prot signaling is required so that bus protection logic can restrict access to this sensitive resource.
evidence.supports_claim: RSA accelerator AXI traffic is the protected resource whose access control depends on correctly set prot fields.

evidence.description: No conditional logic or privilege-level input is applied when assigning rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot; both are pure constant tie-offs with no guard.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard that propagates the actual requester privilege or security level into the AXI protection fields.

findings.reasoning_summary: The source snippet explicitly shows that rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot are both assigned the constant '0. AXI4 protection encoding requires these bits to reflect the privilege level (bit 0), security domain (bit 1), and access type (bit 2) of the initiating master. Hard-coding them to zero means all RSA bus transactions will always appear as unprivileged, secure, data-type accesses to any downstream protection checker, regardless of the true initiator. This can allow a less-privileged or wrong-security-domain initiator to bypass bus-level access controls that inspect the prot field, or conversely can deny a legitimate privileged initiator if protection logic expects prot to reflect actual privilege. The lock field is also tied to zero, preventing any exclusive-access semantics. Together these tie-offs represent a missing guard on bus-level privilege and security signaling for a cryptographic peripheral.
findings.security_impact: A downstream bus firewall or TrustZone-aware interconnect that enforces access control based on AXI prot bits cannot correctly restrict or audit access to the RSA accelerator. An unprivileged software agent whose AXI transactions would otherwise be blocked may have its requests forwarded as if they originated from a privileged, secure master, potentially enabling unauthorized access to RSA key material or control registers.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream memory protection unit or bus firewall configuration is not included in the chain context, so it is not confirmed that any checker actively evaluates the prot field. The impact therefore depends on the system integration. Additionally, if the RSA wrapper converts AXI-lite internally and the upstream bus ignores prot for this peripheral, the practical exposure may be limited; however the code comment explicitly notes these are tie-offs, confirming the fields are not propagated from a privilege-aware source.

findings.summary: The rst_wrapper, which controls reset outputs for multiple system components, accepts AXI requests through axi_lite_interface without any privilege-level or lock guard enforced inside the AXI interface layer itself. The reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs are passed to rst_wrapper as port connections, but the axi_lite_interface used inside rst_wrapper processes all incoming AXI transactions (reads and writes) unconditionally—there is no check within axi_lite_interface that consults reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl before asserting en_o, we_o, or address_o. The same shared axi_lite_interface module is used across rst_wrapper, acct_wrapper, and reglk_wrapper with no access-control gating inside the AXI state machine.

evidence.description: The axi_lite_interface state machine (lines 56–135) processes IDLE, READ, WRITE, and WRITE_B states entirely based on AXI handshake signals (aw_valid, ar_valid, w_valid, r_ready, b_ready). It asserts en_o and we_o unconditionally upon valid AXI transactions. There is no port for reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl and no privilege check inside this module.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI interface layer that serves rst_wrapper has no access-control guard; any initiator that can drive a valid AXI transaction will have en_o/we_o asserted.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i as inputs and outputs multiple reset signals (rst_1 through rst_8+). These reset outputs affect system-wide security-sensitive components. The axi_req_i input is passed directly into axi_lite_interface with no interposition of the lock controls before the AXI interface is serviced.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset controller is a security-sensitive resource; its AXI-accessible registers should be gated by register-lock and access-control signals before the AXI interface commits a transaction.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper instantiates axi_lite_interface_i (line 128) and connects axi_req_i directly to it. The reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i signals present in rst_wrapper's port list (SNIP-0010) are not shown gating the AXI interface before or inside axi_lite_interface; the en/we signals come directly from the state machine without consulting those controls.
evidence.supports_claim: Write and read access to reset-control registers can proceed without enforcement of the register-lock or access-control permissions at the AXI interface level.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals connects rst_axi_req directly to i_rst_wrapper and passes reglk_ctrl[12*8+7:12*8] and acct_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12] as side-band controls. However, these controls are not wired into the axi_lite_interface inside rst_wrapper to gate AXI transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: The instantiation shows that privilege/lock information exists at the top level but is not enforced inside the AXI transaction path.

evidence.description: rst_axi_req AXI fields (aw.prot, ar.prot, aw.lock, ar.lock) are all tied to zero (lines 990–1003). AXI protection (prot) and lock signals that could convey privilege level to the slave are permanently cleared, so no privilege information reaches the axi_lite_interface even via the AXI bus.
evidence.supports_claim: Tying aw.prot and ar.prot to zero means the AXI interface receives no privilege-level signal through the standard AXI protection mechanism, compounding the missing guard.

findings.reasoning_summary: The axi_lite_interface module used inside rst_wrapper contains a state machine that grants read and write access (en_o, we_o, address_o) to any initiator that presents valid AXI handshake signals. No reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl gating exists inside this module. At the top level, rst_axi_req.aw.prot and rst_axi_req.ar.prot are tied to zero, stripping any AXI-native privilege signaling. The reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i ports on rst_wrapper are not used to gate the AXI interface path before transactions are committed. Consequently, an unprivileged initiator that can drive AXI transactions to the rst_wrapper address range can read and write reset-control registers without any lock or privilege check being enforced.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious AXI master can write to reset-control registers inside rst_wrapper, asserting or de-asserting system resets (rst_1–rst_8+) without authorization. This can cause denial of service, bypass security-critical initialization sequences, or reset components holding security state (keys, counters, access-control memories) at will.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippet for the interior of rst_wrapper beyond line 129 is not fully shown, so it is possible that reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i are applied as a write-enable gate after en_o/we_o are produced by axi_lite_interface. However, based on the available evidence, no such gating is visible in the AXI interface path, and the parallel wrappers (acct_wrapper, reglk_wrapper) show the same pattern with direct pass-through to axi_lite_interface.

findings.summary: AXI protection field (aw.prot and ar.prot) for SHA256 peripheral is hardwired to zero, meaning all accesses to the SHA256 accelerator are permanently marked as unprivileged, non-secure, data accesses with no privilege or security differentiation.

evidence.description: Line 1505 assigns sha256_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and line 1516 assigns sha256_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. The AXI PROT field encodes privilege level (bit 0), security (bit 1), and instruction/data (bit 2). Hardwiring both to zero means the SHA256 peripheral bus interface cannot enforce or signal privilege or security context, disabling any downstream interconnect protection that relies on PROT signaling.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields of the SHA256 AXI request struct are unconditionally tied to 0, permanently removing privilege and security attribute propagation for all accesses to the SHA256 cryptographic accelerator.

evidence.description: The sha256_axi_req struct carries all AXI transactions to the SHA256 cryptographic hardware accelerator. Cryptographic peripherals typically require privilege-level gating to prevent unprivileged software from directly accessing hash computation state or keys.
evidence.supports_claim: SHA256 is a security-sensitive cryptographic peripheral whose AXI bus interface should convey proper privilege and security attributes.

evidence.description: No conditional logic or privilege-based gating is present in lines 1499-1518. All AXI sideband fields including lock ('0), cache ('0), prot ('0) are statically tied off without consulting any privilege state register, mode signal, or security context.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard or privilege-level check applied before assigning the protection attributes; the zero assignment is unconditional.

findings.reasoning_summary: The source snippet at lines 1499-1518 shows that the SHA256 AXI request interface is constructed by tying off non-AXI-Lite sideband signals. Critically, both aw.prot and ar.prot are hardwired to zero ('0). In the AXI protocol, the PROT field is the primary mechanism for signaling whether a transaction is privileged, secure, or an instruction fetch. Setting it permanently to zero means every transaction to the SHA256 peripheral is presented to the interconnect as unprivileged and non-secure, regardless of the actual privilege level of the initiating software. Any downstream TrustZone-aware interconnect, firewall, or memory protection unit that gates access to the SHA256 peripheral based on PROT signaling would be bypassed or unable to correctly enforce access control.
findings.security_impact: Any software, including unprivileged user-mode code, can issue transactions to the SHA256 accelerator that appear to the interconnect as unprivileged accesses. If the system relies on AXI PROT bits to enforce that only privileged or secure-world software may access the cryptographic accelerator, this hardwired zero defeats that protection. This could allow unprivileged code to access, observe, or manipulate in-progress hash operations or trigger unauthorized use of the cryptographic hardware.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual security impact depends on whether the downstream interconnect or any memory protection unit uses PROT bits for access control. If the system does not rely on AXI PROT signaling for security enforcement, the practical impact is reduced. The snippet confirms the hardwiring but does not show the interconnect's policy. Additionally, aw.lock is also tied to zero, which disables exclusive access semantics, though this is a separate concern.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper resets all access-control memory entries to 0xFFFFFFFF (fully permissive) on any reset, including the peripheral reset rst_6 which is itself controlled by the rst_wrapper. An attacker who can trigger rst_6 via the rst_wrapper will force acct_mem back to all-ones, bypassing any previously programmed access-control restrictions.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper generates rst_6 which is assigned from rst_mem[6] (the ACCT reset line). The rst_wrapper accepts acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i but also exposes rst_6 as an output that feeds directly into acct_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset line rst_6 targeting the ACCT module is software-controllable through the rst_wrapper peripheral registers.

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, the reset condition is `if(~(rst_ni && ~rst_6))`, meaning either system reset OR rst_6 asserted causes all acct_mem entries to be set to 32'hffffffff (fully permissive / all access granted).
evidence.supports_claim: Triggering rst_6 resets the entire access-control table to the most permissive state, which is a security-critical unsafe reset behavior.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};`. After rst_6 resets acct_mem to all-ones, acc_ctrl_o broadcasts fully permissive access control to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Resetting acct_mem immediately propagates all-permissive access control values to every downstream peripheral access check.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper is instantiated with acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12], gating AXI access to rst_wrapper registers by privilege level. However, a sufficiently privileged (or incorrectly privileged) caller can write the rst_mem to assert rst_6.
evidence.supports_claim: The access guard on rst_wrapper is based on the same acc_ctrl mechanism that gets wiped by rst_6, creating a potential circular weakening of the protection.

evidence.description: The reset value of acct_mem is 32'hffffffff for every entry. There is no separate secure-boot or one-time-programmable initialization path; rst_6 can be triggered post-boot to erase all access restrictions and restore full access, bypassing whatever the firmware had programmed.
evidence.supports_claim: A software-triggered peripheral reset (rst_6) can be used to wipe all access-control policy programmed into acct_mem, constituting a bypassed lock/privilege check.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper stores per-peripheral access-control bits in acct_mem. On reset (either global rst_ni or the peripheral-specific rst_6), these bits are all set to 0xFFFFFFFF, meaning all access is granted. The rst_6 signal is produced by rst_wrapper based on software-writable rst_mem registers. The access guard on rst_wrapper uses acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12], which is itself derived from the acct_mem that can be wiped. An attacker who manages a write to rst_wrapper (even once, at a privileged moment, or if the acct entry for peripheral 12 is still all-ones at boot) can assert rst_6, which resets acct_mem back to all-ones, granting all peripherals full access regardless of any previously configured policy.
findings.security_impact: Complete bypass of peripheral access control: by triggering rst_6 through rst_wrapper, all acct_mem entries revert to 0xFFFFFFFF, granting every privilege level access to every peripheral. This undermines the entire hardware-enforced isolation model provided by the ACCT module.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full rst_wrapper write logic (showing exactly how rst_mem[6] is set) is partially captured; the snippet shows rst_mem[13] and a default `rst_mem <= 0`, but the case statement body for rst_mem[6] is not included in the provided snippets. The overall pattern is clear from what is available, but the exact write condition for rst_6 would confirm exploitability with certainty.

findings.summary: The en_acct signal in rst_wrapper (and similarly in acct_wrapper and reglk_wrapper) is driven directly from the AXI lite interface's en_o without gating by the acct_ctrl_i access-control input, allowing unauthenticated AXI transactions to enable register reads/writes to the reset controller regardless of the privilege-level-based access control.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper declares en and en_acct as internal signals sourced from the axi_lite_interface en_o output. These signals gate access to the reset control registers. The declaration shows en_acct is present but its relationship to acct_ctrl_i is not enforced here.
evidence.supports_claim: The en/en_acct signals are the sole access gate for register reads and writes inside rst_wrapper.

evidence.description: axi_lite_interface sets en_o=1 unconditionally whenever a write transaction is valid (WRITE state with w_valid) or a read is presented (READ state). There is no check of any acct_ctrl_i or privilege-level signal inside axi_lite_interface; the module has no such input port at all.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI lite interface that drives en_o (mapped to en_acct) has no access-control gate, so en_acct is asserted for any AXI transaction regardless of privilege level.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i (privilege-level-qualified access control) and reglk_ctrl_i (register lock bits) as inputs, but these are only forwarded to the internal rst_ctrl logic for write protection of individual registers. The acct_ctrl_i is not used to gate en_acct before it reaches the AXI lite interface.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_ctrl_i is received but not wired to suppress or gate en_acct, leaving the access-enable unprotected.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper is instantiated with acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12], which is the privilege-level-qualified access control bit. Despite this correct hookup at instantiation, the internal logic of rst_wrapper does not use acct_ctrl_i to block AXI enable (en/en_acct), so the privilege check is ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: The intent is to restrict reset controller access by privilege level, but the guard is not actually enforced on the enable path.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper similarly receives acct_ctrl_i but en_acct is wired directly from axi_lite_interface en_o with no gating by acct_ctrl_i. The access-control signal is accepted as an input but never applied to restrict the en_acct enable signal that permits register reads.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: acct_ctrl_i is not used to gate en_acct, so any AXI master can read/write acct_mem regardless of privilege level.

evidence.description: The read path in acct_wrapper checks only reglk_ctrl bits before returning acct_mem contents (e.g., rdata = reglk_ctrl[4] ? 'b0 : acct_mem[0]). There is no check on acct_ctrl_i or en_acct being qualified by privilege level. A low-privilege AXI master that can reach the acct_wrapper address range will have en asserted and can read access-control memory.
evidence.supports_claim: The acct_ctrl_i privilege gate is bypassed; reglk_ctrl is the only protection, and it is independently controllable.

findings.reasoning_summary: The design intends to restrict access to the reset controller (rst_wrapper), access-control table (acct_wrapper), and register lock module (reglk_wrapper) based on the current privilege level via acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral_index]. However, inside each wrapper, acct_ctrl_i is declared as an input but is never used to gate the en or en_acct signal that is driven from the axi_lite_interface en_o. The axi_lite_interface has no knowledge of privilege level and asserts en_o for any valid AXI transaction. As a result, any bus master that can route an AXI request to these peripherals can read or write their registers, bypassing the intended privilege-level access control.
findings.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious AXI master can: (1) write to acct_mem in acct_wrapper to escalate its own access control permissions for other peripherals; (2) write to reglk_mem in reglk_wrapper to unlock or lock register protection bits; (3) trigger arbitrary peripheral resets via rst_wrapper. This represents a complete bypass of the privilege-level-based access control enforcement, enabling privilege escalation and denial-of-service.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full write-side logic of rst_wrapper and reglk_wrapper for the use of acct_ctrl_i is not shown in the snippets (only rst_wrapper lines 105-129 and the port declarations are visible). It is possible acct_ctrl_i is used elsewhere in the write path not captured here, but the read path and en_acct gating absence are clearly evidenced.

findings.summary: The rst_wrapper module accepts reglk_ctrl_i as a register-lock input but the source snippets show that rst_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl_i purely as an input port (SNIP-0011) and the write-side logic in acct_wrapper uses inconsistent bit indices from reglk_ctrl for write-lock vs. read-lock (different bits gate writes versus reads for the same memory regions). More critically, the acct_wrapper reset behavior initializes acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-ones, i.e., all access granted) rather than a secure default of all-zeros, and the reglk_ctrl lock bits that protect acct_mem writes use different bit positions than those protecting reads of the same entries, meaning a partial bypass of the lock is possible: a bit that allows a read can be set while a different bit gates writes, and vice versa.

evidence.description: On reset, acct_mem entries are initialized to 32'hffffffff (all access bits set, i.e., maximally permissive) rather than a secure default of 0. This means any reset event, including an attacker-triggered one via rst_6, leaves the access-control memory in an open state.
evidence.supports_claim: Unsafe reset behavior: reset initializes protected resource to all-ones (open) rather than all-zeros (closed).

evidence.description: Write-lock for acct_mem[0..2] uses reglk_ctrl[5], but read-lock for the same entries uses reglk_ctrl[4]. Write-lock for acct_mem[3..5] uses reglk_ctrl[13], but read-lock uses reglk_ctrl[2]. Write-lock for acct_mem[6..8] uses reglk_ctrl[1], read-lock uses reglk_ctrl[0]. The lock bits are mismatched between read and write paths for each group of registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent lock bit assignment: different reglk_ctrl bits protect reads versus writes of the same acct_mem entries, creating potential for asymmetric lock bypass.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl in acct_wrapper is a 16-bit signal but reglk_ctrl_i input is only 8 bits wide. The assign reglk_ctrl = reglk_ctrl_i wires an 8-bit value into a 16-bit signal, leaving the upper 8 bits at zero. The write-lock for acct_mem[3..5] tests reglk_ctrl[13], which is always zero because it falls in the undriven upper byte — meaning those entries can never be write-locked via this mechanism.
evidence.supports_claim: Structural width mismatch: reglk_ctrl_i is 8 bits but reglk_ctrl is 16 bits; bits 8-15 are always 0, so write-lock checks on bits 13 and above are permanently disabled.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper outputs rst_1 through rst_13, controlling reset of all security-sensitive peripherals (AES, SHA256, HMAC, PKT, ACCT, UART, DMA, etc.). These reset signals are gated by reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i but the width mismatch in the consumer (acct_wrapper) means the lock protection of the access-control memory that governs peripheral access can be bypassed.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset controller for security peripherals relies on the access-control memory, which has the identified protection weaknesses.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i connected to reglk_ctrl[6*8+7:6*8], which is an 8-bit slice, confirming the 8-bit input feeding a 16-bit internal signal — upper byte permanently zero.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms that bits 13 and higher of internal reglk_ctrl in acct_wrapper are always 0, disabling write-lock for acct_mem[3..5].

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper module contains two concrete vulnerabilities supported by source code: (1) On reset, acct_mem is initialized to 0xFFFFFFFF instead of 0, leaving the access-control memory in a maximally permissive state after any reset event including attacker-controlled resets via rst_6. (2) The reglk_ctrl_i input is 8 bits wide but the internal reglk_ctrl signal is 16 bits; the upper byte is never driven (stays 0). Write-lock checks for acct_mem[3..5] test reglk_ctrl[13], which is always 0, permanently defeating write protection for those entries. Additionally, the read-lock and write-lock for each group of acct_mem entries use different reglk_ctrl bit indices, creating an asymmetric protection scheme where holding a read lock does not imply a write lock and vice versa.
findings.security_impact: An attacker who can trigger a reset (via rst_6, which is driven by rst_wrapper) gains an acct_mem containing all-ones access permissions. Additionally, the write-lock for acct_mem[3..5] (controlling access to peripherals indexed 3-5) is permanently disabled due to the 8-to-16-bit width mismatch, allowing any authorized AXI write to overwrite those access-control entries regardless of the intended lock state. This undermines the entire peripheral access-control policy enforced by acct_wrapper.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full rst_wrapper write-side logic for rst_mem is partially shown (lines 105-110 of SNIP-0012 show only the tail of the case statement). The precise conditions under which rst_6 is asserted are not fully visible, but the reset-to-all-ones behavior in acct_wrapper is unconditionally present in SNIP-0007 regardless of how rst_6 is generated.

findings.summary: The rst_wrapper module resets rst_mem to 0 unconditionally whenever 'start' is not asserted (else branch), meaning peripheral reset signals are continuously de-asserted unless actively driven high each cycle. Additionally, the 'start' and 'rst_id' inputs that control which peripheral is reset are guarded only by a single reglk_ctrl_i bit and acct_ctrl_i, but when rst_12 (self-reset of RST_CTRL) is asserted, both start and rst_id are zeroed, potentially allowing a peripheral reset sequence to be interrupted or cleared. More critically, the reset memory register (rst_mem) falls to 0 in the else branch every cycle when start is low, creating an unsafe reset behavior where reset pulses are only one-cycle wide and cannot be sustained, which could lead to improperly short or missed resets for security-sensitive peripherals.

evidence.description: Lines 107-109: The else branch unconditionally clears rst_mem to 0 every clock cycle when 'start' is not asserted. This means reset signals to all peripherals (AES, SHA256, HMAC, PKT, DMA, RNG, RSA, etc.) are only one cycle wide unless 'start' is held high continuously.
evidence.supports_claim: Unsafe reset behavior: peripheral reset signals (rst_1 through rst_13) are automatically cleared every cycle when start is low, making reset pulses ephemeral and potentially ineffective for security-sensitive peripherals.

evidence.description: Lines 58-70: All peripheral reset signals (rst_1=AES0, rst_2=AES1, rst_3=SHA256, rst_4=HMAC, rst_5=PKT, rst_6=ACCT, rst_7=UART, rst_8=DMA, rst_9=REGLK, rst_10=RNG, rst_11=AES2, rst_13=RSA) are directly driven from rst_mem bits, making rst_mem the single control point for resetting all security-sensitive peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_mem controls reset of all security-sensitive peripherals; its transient clearing in the else branch affects all of them simultaneously.

evidence.description: Line 140: The enable signal 'en' is gated by both en_acct and acct_ctrl_i. Lines 153-161: Write to 'start' and 'rst_id' require en && we. Line 156: start write is also protected by reglk_ctrl_i[1]. However, these guards only protect the write path; the automatic clearing of rst_mem in the else branch (SNIP-0003 line 108) occurs without any privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: Access controls exist for writing start/rst_id but do not protect the automatic clearing of rst_mem in the else branch, undermining the protection mechanism.

evidence.description: Lines 73-110: The always block clears rst_mem to 0 in the else branch (line 108: rst_mem <= 0) whenever neither reset nor start is active. This means that after a single clock cycle of reset assertion (start high, case match), rst_mem[N] goes back to 0 the next cycle when start falls low. A properly secure reset controller should latch the reset signal and hold it until an explicit clear, not automatically clear it every cycle.
evidence.supports_claim: The automatic unconditional clearing of rst_mem when start is low creates a one-cycle reset pulse vulnerability: peripheral reset signals are not sustained, potentially leaving security peripherals in an incompletely reset state.

evidence.description: Lines 918-927: rst_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with reglk_ctrl_i connected to reglk_ctrl[12*8+7:12*8] and acct_ctrl_i to acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][12]. The reset outputs (rst_1 through rst_13) flow directly to peripherals without additional filtering.
evidence.supports_claim: The reset signals generated by the flawed rst_wrapper logic propagate directly to all security-sensitive peripherals in riscv_peripherals.

findings.reasoning_summary: The rst_wrapper module contains an unsafe reset behavior in its always block. When 'start' is not asserted, the else branch (line 108) unconditionally clears rst_mem to 0 every clock cycle. Since rst_1 through rst_13 are continuously driven from rst_mem bits (lines 58-70), a reset to any peripheral is only effective for a single clock cycle - the cycle when 'start' is high and the corresponding case arm sets rst_mem[N] to 1. The very next cycle, if start has gone low, the else branch clears rst_mem back to 0, deassserting the reset. This is architecturally unsound for a security reset controller: a proper implementation should latch the reset state and hold it until explicitly cleared. The access control guards (acct_ctrl_i, reglk_ctrl_i) only protect the write path to 'start' and 'rst_id', not the automatic clearing mechanism. This means that even a privileged, authorized reset operation may not properly reset security-critical peripherals like AES, SHA256, HMAC, RNG, and RSA if the reset pulse is only one cycle long.
findings.security_impact: Security-sensitive peripherals (AES, SHA256, HMAC, RNG, RSA, DMA, REGLK) may receive only a one-cycle reset pulse, which may be insufficient to properly clear their internal state, cryptographic keys, or access control registers. An attacker could potentially exploit this by inducing a reset that appears to have cleared a peripheral but left residual sensitive data (e.g., crypto keys) accessible through subsequent operations.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual timing requirements for reset of each peripheral are not visible in the provided snippets - some peripherals may be designed to respond to single-cycle reset pulses. The full rst_ctrl or rst_wrapper module may have additional logic not shown. The exact security threat model for this SoC is not fully specified. The behavior of downstream peripherals when receiving transient resets is unknown.

findings.summary: The rst_wrapper module resets all peripheral reset signals (rst_mem) to 0 when the system reset (rst_ni) is deasserted, but on reset the access control memory (acct_mem) in acct_wrapper is initialized to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-ones), meaning all access is granted. The rst_12 signal is declared as an internal logic wire in rst_wrapper (not output to riscv_peripherals), creating an asymmetry where one peripheral reset output is missing from the external interface while others are connected. More critically, the acct_wrapper reset behavior sets acct_mem to all-ones (0xffffffff) on reset, which means all access control bits are set, potentially granting full access to all protected resources upon any reset event including a soft reset triggered via the reset controller.
