evidence.description: On reset (when rst_ni is low OR rst_6 is high), acct_mem is set to 0xffffffff for all entries. This means every access control bit is set to 1, granting all permissions. A reset triggered by the rst_wrapper (via rst_6) causes acct_mem to be initialized to all-ones, which represents maximum privilege grant rather than a safe locked-down state.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset of acct_mem sets all access control bits to 1 (0xffffffff), granting all access rather than denying it, on any reset including software-triggered resets via rst_6.

evidence.description: rst_wrapper assigns rst_6 = rst_mem[6] which corresponds to the ACCT peripheral reset. rst_12 is declared as an internal logic wire (line 49) and is not an output port, meaning it is not connected to riscv_peripherals unlike rst_1 through rst_11 and rst_13. This means peripheral 12 cannot be selectively reset via the reset controller.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_12 is an internal-only signal in rst_wrapper without an output port, so it cannot propagate to riscv_peripherals, creating an inconsistency in the reset control chain.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates rst_wrapper with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i guards, but the connection only covers rst_1 through rst_11 and rst_13. rst_12 is absent from the port connections at the riscv_peripherals level, confirming the missing reset output.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_12 is not connected in riscv_peripherals, confirming the asymmetric reset control coverage.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper receives rst_6 as an input and uses it alongside rst_ni in the reset condition. The rst_6 signal originates from rst_wrapper's rst_mem[6], which is software-writable via the AXI interface. This means software can trigger the ACCT module reset, causing acct_mem to be set to all-ones.
evidence.supports_claim: Software-triggered reset via rst_6 causes acct_mem to reset to 0xffffffff, granting all access permissions.

evidence.description: The rst_wrapper's rst_mem is set to 0 when start is not asserted (line 108: rst_mem <= 0), but the reset write logic populates individual rst_mem entries. The AXI interface (en, we signals) directly allows writing to the reset controller without evidence of privilege level checking beyond reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl_i.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset control registers are writable via AXI, and triggering rst_6 causes acct_mem to reset to all-permissive state.

findings.reasoning_summary: The core vulnerability is that when acct_wrapper is reset (either by system reset rst_ni or by the software-controllable rst_6 signal from rst_wrapper), the access control memory acct_mem is initialized to 0xffffffff (all bits set to 1). This represents a fully permissive state rather than a deny-all safe state. An attacker who can trigger a reset of the ACCT peripheral (via the reset controller's AXI interface) can cause all access control permissions to be granted. Additionally, rst_12 is never output from rst_wrapper, creating a gap in the reset control architecture.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access who can write to the reset controller registers (subject to reglk and acct_ctrl guards) can trigger rst_6 to reset the ACCT module, causing acct_mem to be set to all-ones and granting all access control permissions to all peripherals. This effectively bypasses the entire access control mechanism through a reset-based attack.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to write to the reset controller is not fully shown in the snippets. The reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl_i guards on rst_wrapper may prevent unprivileged access. The semantics of acct_mem all-ones (whether it means grant-all or deny-all) is inferred from the field name and structure but not definitively documented in the provided snippets. Full rst_wrapper write logic between lines 65-110 is partially shown.

findings.summary: The acct_wrapper reset condition uses a logical OR of rst_ni (active-low system reset) and rst_6 (active-high peripheral reset) in a way that allows rst_6 alone to reset the access-control memory (acct_mem) to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), effectively granting full permissions to every slot. Since rst_6 is generated by rst_wrapper and is driven from rst_mem[6] without any privilege or lock check, any entity that can trigger a rst_6 assertion can wipe and reinitialize the access-control table to an all-permissive state.

evidence.description: Line 81-85: The reset branch condition is `~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)`, which evaluates to true when rst_6 is asserted (rst_6=1) regardless of the state of rst_ni. When triggered, acct_mem is reset to 32'hffffffff for all entries, meaning every access-control entry grants maximum permission.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting rst_6 alone resets the access-control memory to all-ones, bypassing any reglk_ctrl lock that would otherwise prevent writes to acct_mem.

evidence.description: Line 63: rst_6 is directly assigned from rst_mem[6] in rst_wrapper: `assign rst_6 = rst_mem[6]; // ACCT`. This means rst_6 is software-controllable via the rst_wrapper AXI register interface.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is a software-writable signal, so an attacker with access to the RST_CTRL peripheral can assert it to trigger the insecure reset path in acct_wrapper.

evidence.description: Lines 153-158: The rst_wrapper write path for 'start' and 'rst_id' checks reglk_ctrl_i[1], but there is no check preventing rst_mem[6] from being set by the reset-trigger state machine (lines 108: rst_mem <= 0 on ~rst_ni, and the case statement sets individual rst_mem bits).
evidence.supports_claim: The rst_wrapper does not apply a lock-bit guard when writing rst_mem[6], meaning rst_6 can be asserted without privilege enforcement.

evidence.description: Line 49: acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem entries. If acct_mem is reset to all-ones, acc_ctrl_o will reflect maximum access permissions for all slave interfaces.
evidence.supports_claim: The access-control output driving downstream permission decisions is corrupted when rst_6 forces acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF.

evidence.description: Lines 918-927: rst_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals and its rst_6 output is wired to acct_wrapper's rst_6 input (edge:connects:13, edge:connects:28), confirming the signal path from rst_wrapper to acct_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The rst_6 signal generated by rst_wrapper flows directly into acct_wrapper, establishing the attack path.

findings.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper uses `~(rst_ni && ~rst_6)` as its reset condition, which means rst_6=1 alone (independent of the system reset rst_ni) is sufficient to reset acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (all permissions granted). The rst_6 signal is driven from rst_mem[6] in rst_wrapper, which is a software-accessible AXI peripheral. The rst_wrapper does not apply a reglk_ctrl lock to the rst_mem bits, so any software with AXI access to the RST_CTRL peripheral can set rst_mem[6]=1, causing rst_6 to pulse high, which triggers the reset path in acct_wrapper and overwrites all access-control entries with all-ones. This completely undermines the access-control enforcement provided by acct_mem.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access to the RST_CTRL peripheral can assert rst_6 to reset the access-control memory (acct_mem) in acct_wrapper to all-ones, granting maximum permissions on all slave interfaces. This bypasses the intended register-lock (reglk_ctrl) protection and allows unauthorized access to protected hardware peripherals controlled by the access-control table.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to write to the RST_CTRL AXI peripheral is not shown in the provided snippets. It is also not confirmed whether rst_mem[6] can be set independently by software or only through the state machine reset sequence shown in SNIP-0007. However, the structural connection and the insecure reset condition in acct_wrapper are clearly evidenced.

findings.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition uses a disjunction of rst_ni, jtag_unlock, and rst_9, meaning any one of these signals alone is sufficient to clear all register-lock memory. Specifically, asserting rst_9 (the REGLK-specific reset from rst_wrapper) without any authentication check clears all reglk_mem entries to zero, effectively unlocking all peripheral access controls.

evidence.description: The always block at line 80 resets reglk_mem[0..5] to 0 when `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)` is true.
